title: Audit export handoff: who/when CSV format & delivery SLA
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Jenna Park <jenna.park@acme-payments.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 14 Sep 2027 15:12:00 -0400
Subject: Audit export handoff: who/when CSV format & delivery SLA

Hi Jenna —

Thanks again for taking the call today. Quick recap and proposed next steps so we all have the same expectations around the ‘who/when’ export you requested during discovery:  

- Scope: one-time export of access events (who did what/when) covering the last 12 months for production workspace "acme-payments-prod".
- Format request: CSV with columns [timestamp_utc, actor_type, actor_id (hashed), action, target_type, target_id, resource_path, request_id].
- PII handling: actor_id to be hashed with SHA-256 and a salt (we'll provide salt rotation details) — account identifiers only, no raw email/SSN.
- Timezone: timestamps in UTC with ISO-8601.
- Retention: export delivery does not change platform retention policies; this export is a snapshot for your audit team.
- Delivery SLA & handoff: we can deliver a sample (subset ~1000 rows) within 48 business hours, full export within 5 business days subject to verification and legal sign-off.

Next steps:
1) Jenna: confirm sample-columns OK and whether you need additional metadata (e.g., IP, user_agent).
2) Once confirmed, Marissa and I will produce the sample and a delivery manifest.
3) Laura (cc'd) will work the export and delivery.

Attachments: I’ll ask Laura to attach the sample CSV after the internal prep.

Ben
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.ai | +1 (415) 555-0121

---



From: Jenna Park <jenna.park@acme-payments.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
Date: Wed, 15 Sep 2027 09:03:00 -0400
Subject: Re: Audit export handoff: who/when CSV format & delivery SLA

Ben / Marissa —

Thanks for the clear summary. A couple of clarifications from Acme audit:  

- Column additions: please include an "event_source" field (e.g., api, console, admin-ui) and an "actor_org_role" if available. Not critical but helpful.
- IP addresses: we request redacted IPv4/IPv6 (last octet zeroed) — if exporting, please indicate redaction method in the manifest.
- Hashing: SHA-256 is fine; please confirm that the salt will be stable for the export (we do NOT want running salts). Provide a short note on how you verify non-reversibility.
- Delivery method & security: SFTP pushed to our intake host (sftp.acme-payments.com) is preferred; we will provide a scoped account. Alternatively, a presigned S3 URL (AES-256 at rest) is acceptable.
- Format: CSV is fine; please include UTF-8 BOM and newline = LF.

Can you produce the 1000-row sample with redacted IP and hashed actor_id by end of Thursday 9/16 EOD? Our compliance team wants to review before we open the formal audit ticket.

Thanks,
Jenna Park
Senior Security Analyst, Acme Payments
jenna.park@acme-payments.com | +1 (617) 555-0789

- Sample payload (~1,000 rows) will include: timestamp_utc, event_source, actor_type, actor_id_hashed, actor_org_role (where present), action, target_type, target_id, resource_path, request_id, ip_redacted.
- IP redaction method: zero last octet for IPv4 and replace last 80 bits for IPv6 (we'll note the algorithm in the manifest).
- Salt: we will use a stable export salt (export_salt_vACME1452_20270916) and include the salt identifier (not the secret) in the manifest. Salt will be rotated for future exports, not within this snapshot.
- Verification note: we will include a short readme describing the one-way hashing and the test you can run on a provided sample mapping (only for validation, not to reverse).
- Delivery: we can push to the SFTP host you provide. If you prefer presigned S3, we will send a link with 72-hour expiry and AES-256 server-side encryption.

Timeline: Sample CSV will be uploaded to a secure staging location by Thu, 2027-09-16 18:00 EDT (23:00 UTC). Full export ETA remains 5 business days after sample approval + sign-off.

I’m attaching a mock sample and the export manifest. If these look good, Ben will coordinate the signature and legal acceptance.

Marissa Cole

Subject: Re: Nimbusly launch — PR assets, quote signoff & embargo timing

Thanks Ben — appreciate the clear checklist. Quick answers: 

1) Quote — please use the following, attributed to me:

SOC 2 renewal — evidence staging & capacity uplift coordination
preview:
From: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>
To: Samir Patel <samir.patel@acme.com>
Cc: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 10 Sep 2027 09:18:00 -0700
Subject: SOC 2 renewal — evidence staging & capacity uplift coordination

Hi Samir,

Per our renewal conversation last week I wanted to kick off the evidence staging plan and confirm the capacity uplift you mentioned on the call. High level:
- Renewal window: target attestation cutoff = 2027-10-15
- Evidence staging: we’ll provide a curated package of control artifacts + sampled logs for the audit team
- Capacity: your seat+throughput request was +40% (burst pool + reserved capacity) — Marcus is on the thread to confirm pricing/term changes

Questions for you:
1) Do you want evidence uploaded to your S3 bucket, or do you prefer a secure share via our partner SFTP?
2) What retention window do your auditors require for the exported logs (30 / 90 / 180 days)?
3) Any specific time windows for bulk transfer to avoid production load?

Attachments: soc2_evidence_index.xlsx (index of artifacts we plan to include), redwood_s3_manifest.csv (sample manifest).

If that looks good I’ll loop in Priya and Karthik for the log export specifics and propose a mutual action plan by EOD Monday.

Thanks,
Alex Martinez
Senior Account Manager | Redwood Inference
alex.martinez@redwood.com
+1 (415) 555-0119

---
Note: We will not share raw customer data without explicit transfer approvals and an ACLed S3 location; see the evidence index for redactions and sampling rules.

From: Samir Patel <samir.patel@acme.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>
Cc: security@acme.com
Date: Sat, 11 Sep 2027 11:07:00 -0700
Subject: Re: SOC 2 renewal — evidence staging & capacity uplift coordination

Alex,

Thanks — this is helpful. A few clarifying items from the audit side:
- Retention: auditors want 90 days of raw audit logs plus a 12-month index for control events. If you can provide the sampled raw logs for 90 days that should be sufficient.
- Transfer: prefer S3 upload to our acct (acme-security-bucket) with bucket policy you provide for write-only uploads. Please include SHA256 checksums in the manifest.
- Timing: bulk transfer preferably outside 02:00–06:00 PDT to avoid backup windows.

Also confirm whether your packaged artifacts will include the change-history for access control lists (ACLs) — auditors ask for an ACL timeline.

Can we do a 30-minute sync next week (Tue/Wed) to finalize the MAP and sign-off on the term amendment?

Regards,
Samir Patel
Head of Security Compliance | Acme Corp
samir.patel@acme.com
+1 (212) 555-0199

From: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>
To: Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 13 Sep 2027 08:45:00 -0700
Subject: Fwd: SOC 2 renewal — evidence staging & capacity uplift coordination

Team — forwarding Samir's notes. Requests we need to cover:
- S3 write-only upload to acme-security-bucket, include SHA256 checksums
- 90 days raw logs + 12-month event index; sampling rules noted in the index
- Avoid transfers 02:00–06:00 PDT
- Confirm ACL change-history export (auditor asked explicitly)

Marcus: can you confirm the commercial path for the +40% seat/throughput uplift and whether they want term extension or monthly commit?
Priya: can you confirm what redactions we need to apply to artifacts vs. provide raw?
Karthik: can you propose the log export format and an estimated transfer size so we can schedule a low-impact window.

I’ve penciled a tentative sync with Samir on Tue Sep 14 at 10:00 PDT — please flag if that conflicts.

Thanks — Alex

---
Alex Martinez
Senior Account Manager | Redwood Inference

From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus.lin@redwood.com>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.com>, Priya Natarajan <priya.natarajan@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 13 Sep 2027 14:32:00 -0700
Subject: RE: Fwd: SOC 2 renewal — evidence staging & capacity uplift coordination

Quick technical plan from infra:
- Export format: gzipped JSONL for event logs (schema v2), CSV for ACL timeline, and a lightweight index (parquet) for auditor queries.
- Retention/export window: we can produce a contiguous 90-day raw dump + a 12-month event index.
- Estimated size: ~1.8 TB for 90 days (based on their current ingestion; we’ll provide a sampled 48 hr export to validate).
- Transfer options:
  1) Direct S3 put to acme-security-bucket (recommended). We will create a short-lived write role and pre-signed manifests. SHA256 included per object.
  2) Encrypted SFTP drop (for compliance teams that block external buckets).
- Impact: bulk export will consume transient egress and CPU; to keep prod steady we propose a 3-night staggered window (3 x 8-hour windows) outside 02:00–06:00 PDT.
- ACL history: we can export a changelog (CSV) going back 18 months, with user ids and timestamps.

Next steps I can own: produce a 48-hour sample export by Wed 15 Sep, provide exact object counts + signed role for S3. ETA: sample by 2027-09-15 17:00 PDT.

Karthik Iyer
Senior Infrastructure Engineer | Redwood Inference
karthik.iyer@redwood.com
Attachment: logs_export_sample.tar.gz (sample 48-hour export, gzipped JSONL sample + manifest)

   - Redwood will deliver: soc2_evidence_index.xlsx (artifact list, redaction notes), redwood_s3_manifest.csv (object-level manifest with SHA256), and logs_export_sample.tar.gz (48-hr sample).
   - Delivery method: direct S3 upload to acme-security-bucket using a short-lived write role; Karthik to create the role and share IAM details.
   - Timeline: sample by 2027-09-15; full 90-day export staged across 2027-09-22 to 2027-09-24 (3 nights).

2) Capacity & commercials
   - Acme confirmed +40% seat uplift (reserved capacity) with a 12-month term extension. Marcus will follow up today with the amendment draft and DocuSign link for signature.

3) ACL history & control artifacts
   - Redwood to include ACL changelog CSV (18 months) and change-history for access control lists as requested by auditors. Priya will confirm redaction rules where PII is present.

4) Audit logistics
   - Audit team will receive parquet index files for quick queries. Redwood will preserve checksums and provide MD5/SHA256 manifests for verification.

Open items:
- Marcus to send the commercial amendment + DocuSign today.
- Priya to confirm PII redaction checklist by 2027-09-16.

Links & attachments: soc2_evidence_index.xlsx, redwood_s3_manifest.csv, logs_export_sample.tar.gz.

title: Mutual Action Plan: embedded activation, canary & training surge
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Daniel Park <dpark@luminahealth.com>, Lumina Health Onboarding <onboard@luminahealth.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Vanessa Ortiz <vanessa.ortiz@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 12 Sep 2027 09:22:00 -0700
Subject: Mutual Action Plan: embedded activation, canary & training surge

Hi Daniel —

Attached is a first-pass Mutual Action Plan (MAP) that embeds the post-signature implementation milestones we discussed: rollout windows, a canary approach, migration cutover steps, and the training surge for your ops and CS teams. Key asks from our side are noted in the \"Action\" column.

High-level proposal (dates are placeholders until you confirm availability):
- Week 0 (Sep 20): Provisioning & network validation (VPC peering + KMS handshake)
- Week 1 (Sep 27): Canary 1 (5% traffic, smoke tests, 48-hour validation)

title: Performance risk holdback & liquidated amounts - Redwood Inference contract
preview:
From: Elena Vega <elena.vega@acme-health.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: contracts@acme-health.com
Date: Sun, 12 Sep 2027 09:21:00 -0700
Subject: Performance risk holdback & liquidated amounts - Redwood Inference contract

Ben —

Thanks for the productive demo last week. Legal and procurement reviewed Redwood’s standard SLO/credit model and asked us to propose additional contractual protections for a healthcare deployment. High level asks:

- Escrowed holdback equal to 3% of ACV (to be released after 12 months unless material breach)
- Tiered liquidated amounts for repeat Severity 1 incidents (first occurrence: credit only; second occurrence within 12 months: 25% of holdback payable; third occurrence: termination right and remaining holdback paid to customer)
- Immediate termination right for any continuity event >72 hours impacting >60% of traffic

We understand this is a heavier ask than credits; Acme’s regulator requires demonstrable financial recourse for prolonged outages. Attached is a draft risk schedule we’d like Redwood to review.

Can you confirm whether Redwood could accept an escrowed holdback + a staged fee-at-risk model, or whether this must be replaced by alternate remedies (e.g., performance bond or expanded credits)? We’re trying to avoid a long legal loop if possible.

Thanks — happy to hop on a quick call.

— Elena Vega
Director, Vendor Risk & Contracts
Acme Health

Attachment: Acme_Risk_Schedule_v1.pdf


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Elena Vega <elena.vega@acme-health.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>
Date: Sun, 12 Sep 2027 11:02:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Performance risk holdback & liquidated amounts - Redwood Inference contract

Elena —

Appreciate the clear ask and the draft schedule. Short answer: we hear the regulatory concerns and want to be constructive, but immediate escrow + unconditional liquidated payments and a hard 72-hour termination will be challenging for us as written.

Proposed next steps from our side:
- I’ve looped in Laura (legal) to outline a counterproposal that balances regulatory comfort with commercial predictability.
- Alternate mechanisms we can explore quickly: capped staged fee-at-risk tied to severity bands, a time-boxed cure period before termination, or a third-party performance bond in lieu of cash holdback.
- We can also offer an expanded monitoring/audit clause and a joint runbook to reduce probability of long outages.

Would Acme consider (a) capping the cash-at-risk to a defined percentage of ACV, and (b) moving to a staged cure and remediation ladder before termination? If so I’ll ask Laura to send concrete language.

Happy to do a 30-min call tomorrow to go through tradeoffs.

Thanks,
Ben
Enterprise Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.ai | +1 415-555-0193


From: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>
To: Elena Vega <elena.vega@acme-health.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.ai>
Date: Mon, 13 Sep 2027 08:45:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Performance risk holdback & liquidated amounts - Redwood Inference contract

Elena —

Thanks for sharing the draft. I’ve reviewed the proposed escrow + liquidated payment structure and here are Redwood’s primary concerns and a compromise path we think is reasonable: 

1) Uncapped immediate payments: We cannot agree to automatic payouts of escrow without a cure opportunity. We propose:
   - Tiered SLO framework with monetary exposure capped at 3% of ACV total (not per event).
   - Monetary exposure is disbursed only after a defined remediation opportunity (30 days for first Severity 1, 14 days for subsequent events within 12 months), or if Redwood fails to meet remediation milestones we will pay into escrow per a pre-agreed schedule.

2) Termination trigger: Automatic termination for any single event >72 hours is extremely disruptive operationally. We propose instead:
   - If a single continuity event exceeds 72 hours and impacts >=60% traffic, Customer can initiate a mandatory CURE window of 7 days. If remediation milestones during that window are not met, Customer may elect termination.

3) Escrow vs Performance Bond: To address regulator comfort while minimizing operational cash on Redwood’s side, we can accept a third-party performance bond (or letter of credit) sized equivalently to the requested holdback, or Redwood will place funds in escrow at contract execution if Acme requires it.

4) Repeat incidents and escalations: For repeat Severity 1 occurrences within a rolling 12-month period, monetary exposure increases on a stepped basis but remains capped at the 3% ACV maximum. We’ll include a right to seek a neutral auditor to validate incident severity and root cause before payout.

Attached is a redlined counter-table that maps severity bands to cure periods and payout triggers. It’s a starting point — we can tighten numbers in the next exchange.

If this framework is acceptable in principle, Ben and I can prepare a clean contract amendment and a DocuSign envelope for signature. We can also route the performance bond option through our finance team if Acme prefers that route.

Regards,
Laura Bennett
Senior Counsel, Commercial Agreements | Redwood Inference
laura.bennett@redwood.ai

Attachment: redwood_counter_risk_table.xlsx


From: Elena Vega <elena.vega@acme-health.com>
To: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 14 Sep 2027 10:15:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Performance risk holdback & liquidated amounts - Redwood Inference contract

Laura / Ben —

Thank you both — the counterproposal is much closer to what Acme can accept. A few clarifications before we proceed to contract language: 

- The 3% ACV cap is acceptable, provided that successive incidents escalate payout steps in a way that makes remediation economically meaningful.
- The 7-day cure window is reasonable, but for any event where remediation fails and the outage has persisted >72 hours we will reserve the right to immediate suspension of new workload onboardings pending remediation (not immediate full termination).
- Acme would prefer the performance bond route; please send details on acceptable instrument types and beneficiary language.

Please include the following in the amendment: HubSpot deal id (we have it as 67890), an explicit neutral auditor clause (NIST-aligned audit standard), and DocuSign routing to contracts@acme-health.com. I’ve attached a short note that summarizes Acme’s internal signoff requirements.

If you can circulate a clean amendment + DocuSign by end of day tomorrow we’ll aim to execute.

— Elena

Attachment: acme_signoff_notes.docx


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Elena Vega <elena.vega@acme-health.com>
Cc: Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>, Rishi Malhotra <rishi.malhotra@redwood.ai>
Date: Tue, 14 Sep 2027 14:30:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Performance risk holdback & liquidated amounts - Redwood Inference contract

Elena —

Great — we’ll proceed on the performance bond route unless Acme insists on an actual escrow. Next steps I’m initiating now: 

1) Laura will send a clean contract amendment (including the 3% ACV cap, stepped payout ladder, 7-day cure, neutral auditor language aligned to NIST and the HUBSpot deal id).
2) I’ll create a DocuSign envelope addressed to contracts@acme-health.com and include the hubspot deal link for reference.
3) Finance is pulling acceptable performance bond templates; acceptable instrument types are: standby letter of credit, surety bond, or bank performance bond. We will provide beneficiary wording that names Redwood Inference and references the amendment.

Links:
- HubSpot deal: https://app.hubspot.com/contacts/12345/deal/67890
- Draft DocuSign template (for signature routing): https://docusign.com/templates/5a2b3c
- Shared folder with counter redlines: https://drive.redwood.example.com/teams/sales/acme-contracts/slot-PRH

If Acme confirms performance bond is preferred I’ll mark the DocuSign envelope accordingly. Expect the draft amendment from Laura within 24 hours. Thanks for the fast turnaround — we’ll aim to keep the legal loop short.

Best,
Ben
Enterprise Account Executive | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.ai | +1 415-555-0193

title: Request: temporary SLA carveout and routing override for model v5.2 after sustained tail-latency increase
preview:
From: Nova Labs <ops@novalabs.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 14 Sep 2027 08:15:00 -0700
Subject: Re: production degradation - model v5.2 tail latency

Hi Ben,

Quick note from our ops team — since late yesterday (09/13) we've seen sustained tail latency increases on the autocomplete path that uses model v5.2. Our telemetry shows p95 rising from ~0.9s to ~1.6s and p99 spikes to ~3s during peak user bursts. This is affecting interactive UX for a small but growing subset of users.

Can Redwood route our production traffic temporarily to the last-known-good variant (v5.1) or enable the fallback routing you mentioned in the onboarding guide? We need a mitigation in place while you investigate the regression. If a short SLA carveout is required so we can accept degraded throughput for a brief window, please advise the steps and any reporting you need from us.

Attached: sample traces and aggregated percentiles from 09/13-09/14 (we'll send a larger dump if helpful).

Thanks,
--
Aisha Mehta
Ops Lead, Nova Labs
ops@novalabs.com



From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
To: Nova Labs <ops@novalabs.com>
Cc: Evelyn Hart <evelyn_hart@redwood.com>, Diego Fernandez <diego_fernandez@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 14 Sep 2027 09:02:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: production degradation - model v5.2 tail latency

Aisha — thanks for the heads up and for the traces. I'm escalating internally and proposing a short mitigation plan:

- Immediate: enable a routing override that shifts 100% traffic from v5.2 -> v5.1 for 30 minutes (hotfix fallback).
- Near-term: run a targeted canary with 10% traffic on a patched v5.2 build to confirm whether kernel/quantization fallback is the cause.
- Investigation: Evelyn and Diego will pick up heatmaps and kernel-level traces to pinpoint the regression.

I'm copying my engineering and ops leads to confirm feasibility and to request a temporary SLA carveout (30–60 minutes) so we can proceed without triggering automatic penalties while we remediate.

Ben
Director, Customer Reliability
Redwood Inference
ben_carter@redwood.com



From: Evelyn Hart <evelyn_hart@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Diego Fernandez <diego_fernandez@redwood.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 14 Sep 2027 10:18:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: production degradation - model v5.2 tail latency

Ben / team — initial findings from a 1-hour look:

- Symptoms: increased variance in batch processing times on node pool svc-infer-7 when sequence length distribution spikes. The runtime shows repeated KV cache evictions for the v5.2 quantized path.
- Hypothesis: a recent kernel selection change (autoselect for int8 kernels) is picking a slower path under certain sequence-length patterns for v5.2. v5.1 uses a conservative kernel fallback and remains stable.

Mitigation options I recommend (in order):
1) Immediate routing override to v5.1 for 30 minutes while we revert the kernel selector flag for v5.2 on canary nodes.
2) Increase the soft batch-size cap on the affected pool to amortize the latency variance during rollback.
3) If routing override is applied, we'll run a controlled 10% rollout of the reverted v5.2 (kernel flag off) to confirm stability before full traffic restoration.

I've attached a small CSV with the percentile deltas and an SLO exception form that Diego can sign off for a short carveout.

Evelyn
Sr. Engineering Manager, Serving Runtime
evelyn_hart@redwood.com



From: Diego Fernandez <diego_fernandez@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>, Evelyn Hart <evelyn_hart@redwood.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>, Nova Labs <ops@novalabs.com>
Date: Tue, 14 Sep 2027 11:04:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: production degradation - model v5.2 tail latency

Agree with Evelyn's plan. Operational notes:

- I can apply the routing override from the control plane. It'll create a short config change that routes traffic to the v5.1 model id used by Nova Labs. The change is reversible and we'll keep a 5-minute audit window.
- Proposed window: 11:30 — 12:00 PT to minimize overlap with your peak.
- I'll create RI-2319 and post logs to the Drive link for your team. Please confirm you want 100% traffic shifted immediately at 11:30.

Attachment: slo-exception-form-v1.pdf (signed pending Ben).

Diego
ProdOps Lead
diego_fernandez@redwood.com



From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.com>
To: Nova Labs <ops@novalabs.com>
Cc: Evelyn Hart <evelyn_hart@redwood.com>, Diego Fernandez <diego_fernandez@redwood.com>, Monica Patel <monica_patel@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 14 Sep 2027 13:22:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: production degradation - model v5.2 tail latency

Aisha — confirms on the plan below:

- We will execute a routing override at 11:30 PT (today) to route Nova Labs production traffic from v5.2 -> v5.1 for 30 minutes.
- Redwood will log the temporary SLA carveout for that period and will not apply automatic SLA deductions; formal SLO exception paperwork is attached and will be recorded under RI-2319.
- Post-mitigation: Evelyn's team will run a controlled 10% canary of v5.2 with the kernel selector reverted and provide a stability report within 3 hours.

Monica — I'll need a short customer note once the override is live so Nova Labs is aware of the rollback and expected restoration timeline.

Ben
--
Ben Carter
Director, Customer Reliability | Redwood Inference
ben_carter@redwood.com
Attachments: latency-analysis-20270914.csv, slo-exception-form-v1.pdf

title: Whitestone commitment: tokens/sec surface model + safety buffer & latency slicing
preview:
From: Ryan Wallace <ryan@whitestoneai.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>
Cc: Whitestone Procurement <procurement@whitestoneai.com>
Date: 2027-09-11T15:04:00-07:00
Subject: Pilot -> Commit: expected steady-state traffic and SLOs

Ben —

Thanks for the pilot run yesterday. High level: Whitestone expects to move a portion of production to Dedicated in Q4. Before we lock a commit, procurement needs a short compute sizing doc (tokens/sec + latency fit) to justify the monthly commitment to our finance team.

Quick facts from our side: average prompt+response token length ~48 tokens, peak concurrent requests observed 220 r/s in a 10-minute spike, average sustained during business hours ~55 r/s. Desired user-facing SLO is p95 latency < 450ms for 95% of calls (we can tolerate higher for long-form responses).

Can you send a short model that maps tokens/sec to number of GPUs, recommended safety buffer, and how the p95 latency behaves under burst vs sustained load? Procurement also asked for an attachment-friendly spreadsheet.

—Ryan
Product, Whitestone AI


From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>
To: Ryan Wallace <ryan@whitestoneai.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>, Connor O'Brien <connor_obrien@redwood.ai>, Whitestone Procurement <procurement@whitestoneai.com>
Date: 2027-09-11T16:22:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Pilot -> Commit: expected steady-state traffic and SLOs

Ryan —

Thanks for the details; attaching a compact model (whitestone_compute_model.xlsx) and a short write-up below. High-level summary up front:
- Baseline sustained tokens/sec target (derived): 55 r/s * 48 tokens = 2,640 tokens/sec.
- Peak observed burst surface: 220 r/s * 48 tokens = 10,560 tokens/sec.
- Model we used for projections: Redwood-13B-q4 (production-optimized quantized variant). Empirical throughput: ~1,150 tokens/sec per A100-80GB equivalent when serving steady microbatched requests (batch factor ~4). H100s show ~1.6x in our tests; we'll show both.
- Safety buffer recommendations: 35% headroom for committed capacity (accounts for diurnal variability + KV cache cold starts), with an optional 20% burst pool for short spikes.
- Recommended committed fleet: 8 A100-equivalent GPUs (sustained surface ~9,200 t/s) to cover peak with scaling/fallback, or 6 H100-equivalents for same sustained. With a 35% committed buffer the committed target becomes ~3,564 t/s (sustained customer baseline); see spreadsheet for exact math.

Attached calculations + quick notes on latency slicing (how p95 shifts with batching and token length). Let us know if you want us to swap the model baseline to an LLM flavor you run in prod — we can regen numbers.

Next step: if procurement is comfortable with the numbers, we can route a draft term sheet to signatures and thread in Finance for monthly commit tiers.

Cheers,
Ben Carter
Enterprise Sales, Redwood Inference
(attachment: whitestone_compute_model.xlsx)

From: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>
To: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>, Connor O'Brien <connor_obrien@redwood.ai>
Date: 2027-09-11T17:05:00-07:00
Subject: Internal: sanity-check the throughput math before we send final

Ben / Connor —

Quick pass on the sheet: a couple of clarifications we should send back to Whitestone before procurement signs off:
1) Clarify model variant and cold-start assumptions. Ben's note assumes warmed KV cache; if Whitestone expects cold-starts for a significant fraction of calls, recommend increasing buffer to 45%.
2) Spell out burst runway in seconds for the 220 r/s spike — current calc shows 10,560 t/s for ~10 minutes. If burst lasts longer than 3–5 minutes we should propose autoscale triggers to avoid throttling.
3) Add a short row showing p95 latency at 2,640 t/s vs 10,560 t/s for both A100 and H100 equivalencies.

I'll add the p95 latency curves to the second tab and a one-line explainer for Finance. Connor — please confirm the per-GPU throughput numbers you used match the latest perf bench from infra (esp. q4 path).

—Marissa
Sr. Solutions Architect, Redwood Inference


From: Connor O'Brien <connor_obrien@redwood.ai>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>
Date: 2027-09-11T18:01:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Internal: sanity-check the throughput math before we send final

Marissa / Ben —

Checked perf numbers against 2027-08 bench run:
- Redwood-13B-q4: steady single-GPU throughput = 1,150 ± 90 t/s at avg response length 48 tokens (batch factor 3–5). p95 latency at 1,150 t/s ~ 320ms.
- H100-equivalent optimized kernel: ~1,850 ± 120 t/s; p95 ~ 260ms under same conditions.
- Note: multi-GPU sharding adds KV-sync overhead; effective per-GPU throughput drops ~6% at >4-GPU shards for long sequences. Use conservative multiplier when recommending >4 GPUs in a single shard.

I'll update the sheet to include both numbers and a separate column applying a 6% penalty when recommending contiguous shards >4 GPUs. Sending revised sheet.

—Connor
Sr. Infra Performance Engineer, Redwood Inference
(attachment: whitestone_compute_model.xlsx — updated)

From: Ben Carter <ben_carter@redwood.ai>
To: Ryan Wallace <ryan@whitestoneai.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwood.ai>, Connor O'Brien <connor_obrien@redwood.ai>, Whitestone Procurement <procurement@whitestoneai.com>
Date: 2027-09-12T09:08:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Pilot -> Commit: expected steady-state traffic and SLOs

Ryan —

Updated attachment and short recommendation below (see Drive link for workbook). Key cells called out for procurement: committed tier suggestion and burst runway.

Recommendation snapshot:
- Committed baseline (covers sustained OH: 2,640 t/s): reserve 6 A100-equivalent GPUs (6 * 1,150 = 6,900 t/s sustained capacity). Why more than the bare baseline? We size committed to the 35% buffer: 2,640 * 1.35 = 3,564 t/s; we map commit tiers to whole GPUs and also account for shard penalty; thus 3,564 -> round to 6 GPUs (gives cushion for expected growth and cache variance).
- Burst & Autoscale: keep an on-demand burst pool (additional 5 GPUs) to absorb short spikes up to 10,560 t/s for 5–8 minutes while auto-scaling completes warm-up.
- Latency expectations: p95 ~ 300–350ms at committed baseline; p95 can rise to 520–700ms during long (minutes+) sustained bursts if burst pool is exhausted. Short spikes should remain under p95 500ms with the burst pool in place.

If that matches your expectations, procurement can sign the term sheet; we can propose a 12-month commit with quarterly checkpoints for traffic review. DocuSign link included in the Drive folder once you confirm.

Best,
Ben

Date: 2027-09-13T09:12:00-07:00
Subject: Re: Pilot -> Commit: expected steady-state traffic and SLOs

Ben / team —

This is very helpful — thank you. Procurement is looped and prefers the 12-month commit with the 6-GPU baseline + burst pool as you outlined. A couple of asks before we circulate to Finance:
- Can you add a one-line item in the spreadsheet that maps the 6-GPU committed monthly charge to tokens-per-month at the 35% buffer (so Finance has a $/token figure)?
- Please include a note about fallback behavior (which model variant we'll fall back to if burst capacity is unavailable) — Whitestone prefers explicit fallback rules in the SLA.

If you add those two bullets, procurement will route to signatures. Appreciate the quick turn.

—Ryan

title: Graybridge: sudden p99 latency spike & errors — proposed KV rebalance
preview:
From: Eli Turner <eli.turner@graybridge.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwood.com>
Cc: Graybridge SRE <sre@graybridge.com>
Date: Sun, 12 Sep 2027 09:14:00 -0700
Subject: Re: API latency spike affecting ingestion pipeline

Hi Aisha —

We started seeing a sharp jump in p99 latency and a correlated rise in 500 responses for the embeddings route around 08:30 PDT. Throughput dropped by ~30% for a 15m window and then oscillated. Our job runs that depend on timely embeddings are backlogged and a downstream ingestion task failed.

Attached is a snapshot from our Grafana (metric_snapshot-20270912.png) and a short log excerpt. We rolled no code on our side in the window. Can you confirm this is on Redwood's side and give an ETA for mitigation? We will escalate internally if this continues.

Thanks,
Eli
Eli Turner
SRE Lead, Graybridge Solutions

Attachments: metric_snapshot-20270912.png (PNG, 86KB)


From: Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwood.com>
To: ops-incident@redwood.com, Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Eli Turner <eli.turner@graybridge.com>
Date: Sun, 12 Sep 2027 09:28:44 -0700
Subject: Fwd: Graybridge: API latency spike affecting ingestion pipeline — urgent triage

Forwarding Graybridge SRE report — they have p99 spikes + 500s on embeddings. Eli says no changes on their side.

Ask: can infra/ops confirm whether we have a KV-store eviction pattern or hot-prefix activity that would cause HOL-style slowdown? If possible, propose a short mitigation (10-30m) we can apply that reduces downstream errors while we run a deeper fix. Please include timing and any customer-facing messaging. I have looped in Marissa for exec awareness.

Aisha
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
--- Forwarded message attached below ---


From: Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwood.com>, ops-incident@redwood.com
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Eli Turner <eli.turner@graybridge.com>
Date: Sun, 12 Sep 2027 09:43:11 -0700
Subject: Re: Graybridge: sudden p99 latency spike & errors — initial triage

Aisha / Eli —

Initial findings from a 09:30 check: multiple tenants were hitting a narrow set of KV prefixes due to a customer-side traffic pattern combined with our recent key-hash change (rolled 09/11). We are seeing rapid TTL churn and eviction storms on a subset of shards which is producing head-of-line queuing for downstream tokenization and batching paths. Symptoms match HOL blocking caused by cache churn rather than pure CPU saturation.

Immediate mitigations we can take (recommended order):
1) Apply an emergency KV rebalance for affected shards (non-disruptive). This spreads hot prefixes across more hosts and reduces eviction contention. Estimated 20m to complete and validate.
2) Temporarily relax prefix hashing affinity for Graybridge's tenant (route their traffic to a dedicated set of shards) — ~10m to flip routing, requires a short config push and circuit check.
3) Reduce batching window for embeddings for the next 30m to avoid large batch stall amplification (config-only, ~5m).

I attached a short runbook and a one-page rebalance plan. If we run 1+2 together we expect p99 to fall within their SLA within ~30 minutes. Risk: rebalance increases behind-the-scenes KV moveload; we will throttle the migration rate.

I will start the rebalance at T+10 assuming ops approves; marking this P1.

Jonas
Principal SRE, Redwood Inference

Attachments: kv_short_runbook.md (Markdown, 6KB), kv_rebalance_plan.pdf (PDF, 38KB)


From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwood.com>, Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Eli Turner <eli.turner@graybridge.com>
Date: Sun, 12 Sep 2027 10:02:05 -0700
Subject: Re: Graybridge: sudden p99 latency spike & errors — exec note

Team —

Thanks for the quick triage. Please proceed with Jonas's recommendations. Aisha, keep Eli in the loop with estimated timing and a short one-line status every 15m. If we cannot return to SLA within 1 hour, I want a suggested credit calculation and an escalation brief for the CRO.

We should also call out the configuration change that correlated with this (09/11 key-hash roll) in the post-mortem and propose a global guardrail to prevent small hash changes from causing shard hotness. Jonas — please snapshot compaction and eviction rates before and after the rebalance for the postmortem.

Marissa Cole
Director, Customer Success | Redwood Inference


From: Aisha Rahman <aisha.rahman@redwood.com>
To: Eli Turner <eli.turner@graybridge.com>
Cc: Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 12 Sep 2027 10:18:52 -0700
Subject: Re: Graybridge: mitigation plan & timing

Eli —

Thanks for flagging this — quick update from our side:
- Jonas is starting an emergency KV rebalance now; we expect the operation to finish and validate within 20–30m.
- We will route your tenant to a temporary dedicated shard set during the window; that flip is in progress.
- We reduced the embeddings batching window for the next 30m to limit stall amplification.

We will send a short status every 15m. If you see any additional 500s, please forward logs and we will add them to the investigation. Sorry for the disruption — we are treating this as a P1 and will follow up with a post-mortem and remediation plan.

Aisha Rahman
Account Executive, Redwood Inference

---
On Mon, 13 Sep 2027 01:05:00 +0000 Jonas Weber <jonas.weber@redwood.com> wrote:
> Update: rebalance completed at 01:05 UTC. p99 reduced from ~2.1s to ~450ms in the affected window; 500s dropped to baseline. Monitoring for 30m.
>
> Jonas

title: Pricing safeguard: benchmark trigger mechanics and implementation
preview:
From: Elena Gross <elena.gross@acmehealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Cc: Procurement Team <procurement@acmehealth.com>
Date: Mon, 5 Sep 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Pricing safeguard: benchmark trigger mechanics and implementation

Hi Ben / Marissa,

Thanks for sharing the draft order form. Our procurement team flagged the new 'benchmark trigger' language in Exhibit B and asked for a short write-up of how it would work operationally. Two quick questions:

- How is the benchmark measured (tokens / API calls / throughput)?
- If a trigger applies, is the adjustment retrospective or forward-looking, and how quickly would it show up on our invoices?

We also saw a potential penalty credit if usage exceeds the cap—can you confirm whether that credit offsets future invoices or is a one-time refund? Procurement would like this clarified before we loop in legal.

Attachment: procurement_rfi_questions.xlsx (stub)

Best,
Elena
Director, Procurement
Acme Health



From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Elena Gross <elena.gross@acmehealth.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 5 Sep 2027 11:03:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Pricing safeguard: benchmark trigger mechanics and implementation

Elena — thanks for the note. Short version below, attaching an annotated redline of Exhibit B for clarity. Happy to jump on a quick call if that helps.

- Measurement: monthly gross API token consumption (gen + system) aggregated per calendar month. Baseline is the committed monthly average in the order form.
- Trigger mechanics: if a month exceeds the benchmark by >25% the pricing floor steps up for subsequent full months until the 3-month moving average falls below the threshold. Changes are forward-looking (apply starting the next billing period).
- Credit: we provide a one-time offset credit to the next invoice if consumption significantly exceeds cap due to service issues (e.g., routing causing duplication). Normal over-usage does not result in cash refunds; it triggers the step-up mechanism instead.

Key trade-offs: forward-looking adjustments keep billing predictable; the moving average avoids ping-pong. The redline adds language for a maximum annual adjustment cap (no more than 18% increase in a 12-month window) and carveouts for scheduled maintenance.

Attachment: pricing_trigger_redline_v3.docx (stub)

Next step: propose a short call with procurement + legal (30 min) this week to align. Are you available Wed 9/7 or Thu 9/8?

Thanks,
Ben
Account Executive, Redwood Inference



From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
To: Elena Gross <elena.gross@acmehealth.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 6 Sep 2027 08:21:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Pricing safeguard: benchmark trigger mechanics and implementation

Adding a couple operational points from CS/RevOps perspective:

- We recommend the 3-month trailing average so temporary spikes (e.g., marketing campaigns) don't immediately re-price Acme.
- Proposal: first trigger is a 10% floor uplift; second consecutive trigger (within 6 months) increases to 15%; beyond that we escalate to a pricing review rather than automatic steps.
- Emergency override: Acme can request an exception for one month per contract year to avoid an automatic step (requires documented incident and joint review).

Jordan, FYI on the invoicing timing — we will implement changes at the invoice cycle boundary (first of next month) and the billing system will show an 'Adjustment due to benchmark trigger' line item.

Marissa
Customer Success Manager, Redwood Inference



From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
To: Elena Gross <elena.gross@acmehealth.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 7 Sep 2027 10:37:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Pricing safeguard: benchmark trigger mechanics and implementation

Elena — Karthik from Legal here. Per Ben's redline I reviewed and suggested edits to tighten definitions and limit operational exposure. Key legal points in the attached redline:

1) Definition clarity — 'Benchmark' must be defined as 'measured monthly gross inference tokens processed by Redwood's platform for Acme's tenant id(s) excluding test and sandbox tokens as reasonably determined by Redwood and reasonably documented to Acme.'
2) Carveouts — planned maintenance windows (>=24h notified) and force majeure events excluded from trigger calculation.
3) Audit & dispute — Acme has the right to request a one-time audit of a triggering month within 60 days; if audit shows Redwood measurement error >=5% then Redwood will adjust charges and issue a corrective credit.
4) Annual cap — no more than 18% aggregate floor uplift in any 12-month period.
5) Transition phrasing — specify adjustments are forward-looking and will be reflected in the first full invoice period following written notice.

I also limited language around 'penalty credit' — we propose 'invoice offset' capped at the greater of (actual measured cost impact OR $20,000) for operational errors attributable to Redwood. That avoids open-ended refunds.

Attachment: exhibit-b_pricing_benchmark_trigger_v1.pdf (redline stub)

Please review; happy to take comments or join the call if legal needs to be present.

Thanks,
Karthik Iyer
Senior Counsel, Redwood Inference



From: Timothy Sloane <timothy.sloane@acmelegal.com>
To: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Elena Gross <elena.gross@acmehealth.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 8 Sep 2027 09:05:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Pricing safeguard: benchmark trigger mechanics and implementation

Karthik — thanks for the redline. A few counterpoints from Acme's counsel:

- Measurement transparency: we need a daily usage report (CSV) available via SFTP within 48 hours of month-end for our internal reconciliation.
- The 18% annual cap is acceptable but we request a lower per-trigger uplift (first trigger 5%, second 10%).
- 'Invoice offset' language: Acme prefers a cash credit (applied to future invoices) with a minimum of $25,000 for verified platform-caused overcharges.
- Audit mechanics: our auditor should be allowed to run a read-only verification against the same metrics Redwood uses; Redwood may redact internal-only fields.

Attached is a marked-up version proposing the above. We'll need to align on sample reporting format before signing.

Attachment: acme_marked_pricing_trigger_comments.docx (stub)

Regards,
Timothy Sloane
Partner, Sloane & Partners (Counsel to Acme Health)



From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Elena Gross <elena.gross@acmehealth.com>, Timothy Sloane <timothy.sloane@acmelegal.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwood.com>
Date: Fri, 11 Sep 2027 15:42:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Pricing safeguard: benchmark trigger mechanics and implementation

Thanks all — constructive thread. Quick summary and proposed next steps so we have alignment before legal redline swaps: 

1) Measurement & report: Redwood will provide a daily usage CSV via SFTP within 72 hours of month-end (we can shorten to 48h once the SFTP is established).
2) Trigger uplift: Redwood accepts a reduced first-trigger uplift of 7.5% and 12.5% on the second trigger within a rolling 6-month window; both subject to the 18% annual cap.
3) Invoice offset: we'll keep the invoice-offset model (applied to future invoices) with a minimum $20k cap; open to discussing a higher floor for enterprise customers on a case-by-case basis.
4) Audit: Acme may request a one-time audit per triggering year; Redwood will provide read-only data feeds for verification with reasonable redactions.

Calls to schedule: suggest Tue 9/14 10:00am PT — procurement + Acme legal + Redwood legal + CS. Ben will send a calendar invite.

Next deliverables: Ben to circulate an updated Exhibit B redline (incorporating Karthik's language + agreed parameter tweaks) by EOD Mon 9/13.

Hey Sam — great to meet you and the Crestline team on the demo earlier. Quick recap and proposed next steps from our side: 

1) Goals we heard: ~1.2M monthly inference tokens initially, scaling to ~6–8M over 9–12 months; strict P50 latency target ~120ms for chat paths; PHI-sensitive content, so private deployment or dedicated lanes with VPC is a preference.

2) Immediate ask from you: an estimate of committed-reserved lane pricing for a 6-month pilot (reserved) and options for H100 vs A100 hardware given the latency/throughput targets. You also mentioned wanting a ballpark $/1M tokens target to compare vs your internal budget.

3) What we’ll deliver by EOD Friday: attached short pricing deck plus a simple cost model Excel with three scenarios (pilot 1.2M, growth 3.5M, scale 7.5M tokens) and per-1M token unit economics for A100 vs H100 reserved lanes.

Questions / asks for you: can you confirm the expected average prompt+generation length (tokens) for the chat flow (ballpark)? Also, who should we loop in on procurement/legal for NDA/PO path — procurement@crestline?

— Aditya Rao
Account Executive | Redwood Inference
aditya@redwood.ai | +1 415-555-0123

Subject: Re: Post-demo recap + next steps on dedicated lane pricing

Thanks Aditya — appreciate the quick follow. Couple clarifications and constraints from our side: 

- Token profile: average request is ~40 tokens prompt + ~220 tokens generation (so ~260 tokens total) for RnR/chat flows. Some short lookups are ~30 tokens total.
- Target throughput: for the pilot we expect bursty traffic with peaks ~45 concurrent sessions; long-term target throughput ~250 concurrent sessions (not all at once; that's cloth-peak over a region) — you can map to tokens/sec if easier.
- Budget guardrails: our procurement sponsor gave an initial guideline of $600–$1,200 / 1M tokens as acceptable band for a reserved pilot evaluation, depending on SLA and data isolation. Lower is better but we'll trade up for dedicated private options.
- Preferred hardware: open to either A100 or H100 but would like you to recommend based on latency and per-1M token economics.

Procurement contact is procurement@crestlinehealth.com — loop them in for contractual path. We’re aiming to start a pilot in mid-Oct if numbers align.

Sam
Senior Product Manager, Crestline Health


From: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.ai>
To: Sam Cho <sam.cho@crestlinehealth.com>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>
Date: Thu, 9 Sep 2027 12:46:10 -0700
Subject: Re: Post-demo recap + next steps on dedicated lane pricing

Thanks Sam — this is super helpful. Quick, high-fidelity response based on your token profile and the budget band you shared. I put the full math in the attached Excel but highlights below: 

Scenario assumptions (using your ~260 tokens/request):
- Pilot: 1.2M tokens/month (roughly ~4.6k requests/month at 260 tokens each)
- Growth: 3.5M tokens/month
- Scale: 7.5M tokens/month

Pricing highlights (reserved dedicated lanes, 6-month commit example):
- A100 lane (single-reserved lane sizing to meet ~120ms P50 for chat): estimated unit = ~$950 / 1M tokens (pilot band), drops to ~$720 / 1M at 3.5M, ~$610 / 1M at 7.5M due to utilization and batching efficiencies.
- H100 lane (preferred if you need headroom for lower latency & higher concurrency): estimated unit = ~$1,150 / 1M (pilot), ~$880 / 1M (3.5M), ~$720 / 1M (7.5M). H100 shows ~18–25% better latency/CPS on our benchmarks for this architecture but has a higher reserved cost.

Where the delta comes from: H100 gives higher throughput per reserved lane (so fewer lanes to buy at scale) but a higher absolute per-hour reserved cost; for small pilots A100 can be cheaper per token because you pay for less idle headroom. We included an intermediate quantized variant and a hybrid route (A100 reserve + H100 burst pool) in the workbook.

Operational notes:
- We can configure automatic fallback routing to a lower-cost quantized variant if utilization spikes and you want to preserve budget.
- Networking: for PHI-sensitivity we recommend Redwood Private (VPC) or Dedicated with customer-managed peering. Procurement impacts pricing; we flagged a compliance add-on in the workbook.

Next steps I propose:
1) Workshop (45–60m) to walk the cost model with procurement + engineering — suggested slots: Tue 14 Sep 10:00am PT or Wed 15 Sep 1:00pm PT.
2) If workshop aligns, we’ll produce a 1-page pilot SOW + PO path and provide a test account for a 2-week performance verification run.

Can you confirm which slot works and whether procurement should join the workshop? If yes, I’ll draft a short invite and share an updated workbook with hybrid-reserve scenarios.

— Aditya

To: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.ai>

title: Multi-factor latency attribution questionnaire — instrumentation & sampling
preview:
From: Diane Huang <diane.huang@stratushealth.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.com>
Cc: Alex Moreno <alex.moreno@stratushealth.com>
Date: Mon, 6 Sep 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: RFI: detailed latency/throughput instrumentation questions

Hi Aditya,

Thanks for the call earlier. As we finalize the SOW for the evaluation, our procurement and infra teams asked for a short, focused questionnaire on how Redwood collects and attributes latency during endpoint benchmarking. Specifically we're trying to understand how to compare your runtime results to our in-house harness — we need:

- Which hop-level timestamps you capture (client, ingress LB, serving runtime, model execution, egress) and whether they are exposed per-request.
- How you sample and aggregate tail quantiles (p95/p99/p99.9) — do you rely on streaming telemetry or batch logs?
- How batching and KV cache hits are instrumented in output metrics.
- Guidance we can use to design an apples-to-apples test harness (what config knobs to control to avoid double-counting network vs token compute).

Can you provide the questionnaire as a short email or attachment we can include in the RFP? We expect to share this with our SRE team and would appreciate explicit examples of the headers/fields we should capture in the harness (timestamps, request ids, kvcache flags).

Thanks,
Diane
Diane Huang
Senior Procurement Manager
StratusHealth

Attachment: none in original message


From: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.com>
To: Diane Huang <diane.huang@stratushealth.com>
Cc: Alex Moreno <alex.moreno@stratushealth.com>, Monica Patel <monica@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 6 Sep 2027 11:08:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: RFI: detailed latency/throughput instrumentation questions

Diane — thanks for the note and the clarity. I put together a compact questionnaire and a short guidance runbook for your SREs. High level first: our goal is to separate transport/network latency from token-serving latency and from internal scheduling/queueing so you can compare numbers across harnesses.

Summary of what we capture (short):
- Client request timestamp (t_client_send) — at your harness client.
- Ingress receive timestamp (t_ingress_recv) — measured at the edge load balancer / API gateway.
- Runtime receive/start timestamp (t_runtime_start) — when the runtime dequeues and begins processing (after batching decision).
- Model inference start/end (t_model_start, t_model_end) — kernel / device-level timestamps where available.
- Egress send timestamp (t_runtime_send) and client receive (t_client_recv).
- KV cache indicators: per-request boolean flag and cache_hit_count.
- Batch size and position: numeric batch_size, ordinal position in batch (0-indexed).
- Fallback indicator: which model variant served the request.

Aggregation & sampling notes (short):
- We recommend streaming per-request traces to a time-series store (e.g., OTLP -> ClickHouse) with a stable request_id for joinability.
- Calculate quantiles on post-processed per-request compute-only latency (t_model_end - t_model_start) and include network-only (t_ingress_recv - t_client_send) as a separate series.
- For tail quantiles use sliding-window rolling aggregation (1h) to reduce transient noise during surges.
- For p99.9 consider reservoir sampling for very high QPS to bound storage while preserving tail fidelity.

I've attached a compact .xlsx with the exact column names we export (one row per request) and a 2-page runbook with an example harness config. Monica and Ben are CC'd to help with follow-ups and any data samples you want us to annotate.

Deliverables attached: rfi_instrumentation_template.xlsx, runbook-sampling.pdf, example-harness-config.yaml

Best,
Aditya Rao
Head of Solutions — Redwood Inference
aditya@redwood.com

---

> Hi Aditya,
>
> Thanks for the call earlier. As we finalize the SOW for the evaluation, our procurement and infra teams asked for a short, focused questionnaire on how Redwood collects and attributes latency during endpoint benchmarking. > - How you sample and aggregate tail quantiles (p95/p99/p99.9) — do you rely on streaming telemetry or batch logs?
> - How batching and KV cache hits are instrumented in output metrics.
> - Guidance we can use to design an apples-to-apples test harness (what config knobs to control to avoid double-counting network vs token compute).
>

>
> Thanks,
> Diane
>


From: Alex Moreno <alex.moreno@stratushealth.com>
To: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.com>
Cc: Diane Huang <diane.huang@stratushealth.com>, Monica Patel <monica@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 7 Sep 2027 08:35:00 -07:00
Subject: Follow-up question: workload shaping and synthetic tokenization

Aditya / team,

Thanks for the materials — we pulled the template into our harness. Two quick clarifications from our infra team before we instrument:

1) Workload shaping: when you run your internal \"gradual ramp\" tests, do you include synthetic long-sequence probes to exercise model attention/kv-cache (e.g., 4% of requests are 4k-token synthetic prompts)? If so, how do you mark/segregate those from live short requests in the datasets?

2) Tokenization parity: our tokenizer maps slightly different token counts to the open model variant. Do you recommend normalizing by token-count (compute-per-token) or by request wall time for cross-harness comparison?

Also we noticed you export a \"batch_position\" field — is that ordinal guaranteed to reflect final batch execution ordering? Our harness uses interleaving and it’s important we attribute per-token time reasonably.

Appreciate a quick pointer so we can finish instrumenting and run an initial comparison next week.

— Alex


From: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.com>
To: Alex Moreno <alex.moreno@stratushealth.com>, Diane Huang <diane.huang@stratushealth.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 9 Sep 2027 15:42:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Follow-up question: workload shaping and synthetic tokenization

Alex — quick answers below, then a short recommended practice you can paste into the RFP.

1) Workload shaping: yes — our internal surge runs include a small proportion (2–5%) of long synthetic sequences to exercise KV cache warmup and attention scaling. We tag those with an explicit synthetic_probe boolean and a synthetic_profile id so they can be excluded from production quantiles or used to produce a separate "long-tail" latency series. We recommend you do the same so you can produce two comparable views: normal traffic + synthetic-probes.

2) Tokenization parity: normalize where possible by effective token-count when reporting compute-per-token metrics (compute_ms_per_token = (t_model_end - t_model_start) / token_count). For end-to-end latency comparisons, report both compute-per-token and wall-time percentiles. That lets you see whether tokenizer differences are causing per-request skew.

3) batch_position: batch_position is the ordinal position in the batch at dequeue time; it is deterministic for the runtime's scheduling policy during that test run but not guaranteed across restarts. For interleaving harnesses capture both batch_id and batch_position; join on request_id to attribute accurately.

Recommended pasteable RFP language (short):

-- Instrumentation fields we expect the vendor to provide (one row per request):
- request_id (string UUID)
- t_client_send (ISO8601)
- t_ingress_recv (ISO8601)
- t_runtime_start (ISO8601)
- t_model_start (ISO8601)
- t_model_end (ISO8601)
- t_runtime_send (ISO8601)
- t_client_recv (ISO8601)
- token_count (int)
- synthetic_probe (boolean)
- synthetic_profile (string)
- cache_hit_count (int)
- batch_id (string)
- batch_position (int)
- model_variant (string)

-- Sampling & aggregation guidance:
- Export per-request traces to a streaming store; compute compute-only and network-only latencies separately.
- Use reservoir sampling for p99.9 when QPS > 20k to bound storage.
- Provide a rolling 1h windowed quantile for operational tail analysis and a full-test-run quantile for gating acceptance criteria.

If helpful we can run a 30-minute cross-harness run with your team and annotate the CSV together. Monica / Ben — can you own scheduling and share the QuickStart CLI command and minimal config? Kimberly — flag any security/ingress constraints for Stratus so we send the proper allowlist.

Thanks — happy to jump on a short 30m call if the SREs want to walk the CSV.

Best,
Aditya

--
Aditya Rao
Head of Solutions — Redwood Inference
aditya@redwood.com
Phone: +1 (415) 555-0192

Subject: Re: RFI: detailed latency/throughput instrumentation questions

Diane — thanks for the note and the clarity. I put together a compact questionnaire and a short guidance runbook for your SREs. High level first: our goal is to separate transport/network latency from token-serving latency and from internal scheduling/queueing so you can compare numbers across harnesses.

Summary of what we capture (short):
- Client request timestamp (t_client_send) — at your harness client.
- Ingress receive timestamp (t_ingress_recv) — measured at the edge load balancer / API gateway.
- Runtime receive/start timestamp (t_runtime_start) — when the runtime dequeues and begins processing (after batching decision).
- Model inference start/end (t_model_start, t_model_end) — kernel / device-level timestamps where available.
- Egress send timestamp (t_runtime_send) and client receive (t_client_recv).
- KV cache indicators: per-request boolean flag and cache_hit_count.
- Batch size and position: numeric batch_size, ordinal position in batch (0-indexed).
- Fallback indicator: which model variant served the request.

Aggregation & sampling notes (short):
- We recommend streaming per-request traces to a time-series store (e.g., OTLP -> ClickHouse) with a stable request_id for joinability.
- Calculate quantiles on post-processed per-request compute-only latency (t_model_end - t_model_start) and include network-only (t_ingress_recv - t_client_send) as a separate series.
- For tail quantiles use sliding-window rolling aggregation (1h) to reduce transient noise during surges.
- For p99.9 consider reservoir sampling for very high QPS to bound storage while preserving tail fidelity.

I've attached a compact .xlsx with the exact column names we export (one row per request) and a 2-page runbook with an example harness config. Monica and Ben are CC'd to help with follow-ups and any data samples you want us to annotate.

Deliverables attached: rfi_instrumentation_template.xlsx, runbook-sampling.pdf, example-harness-config.yaml

Best,
Aditya Rao
Head of Solutions — Redwood Inference
aditya@redwood.com

---

Aditya / team,

Thanks for the materials — we pulled the template into our harness. Two quick clarifications from our infra team before we instrument:

1) Workload shaping: when you run your internal \"gradual ramp\" tests, do you include synthetic long-sequence probes to exercise model attention/kv-cache (e.g., 4% of requests are 4k-token synthetic prompts)? If so, how do you mark/segregate those from live short requests in the datasets?

2) Tokenization parity: our tokenizer maps slightly different token counts to the open model variant. Do you recommend normalizing by token-count (compute-per-token) or by request wall time for cross-harness comparison?

Also we noticed you export a \"batch_position\" field — is that ordinal guaranteed to reflect final batch execution ordering? Our harness uses interleaving and it’s important we attribute per-token time reasonably.

From: Aditya Rao <aditya@redwood.com>
To: Alex Moreno <alex.moreno@stratushealth.com>, Diane Huang <diane.huang@stratushealth.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>, Kimberly Park <kimberly@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 9 Sep 2027 15:42:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Follow-up question: workload shaping and synthetic tokenization

Alex — quick answers below, then a short recommended practice you can paste into the RFP.

1) Workload shaping: yes — our internal surge runs include a small proportion (2–5%) of long synthetic sequences to exercise KV cache warmup and attention scaling. We tag those with an explicit synthetic_probe boolean and a synthetic_profile id so they can be excluded from production quantiles or used to produce a separate "long-tail" latency series. We recommend you do the same so you can produce two comparable views: normal traffic + synthetic-probes.

2) Tokenization parity: normalize where possible by effective token-count when reporting compute-per-token metrics (compute_ms_per_token = (t_model_end - t_model_start) / token_count). For end-to-end latency comparisons, report both compute-per-token and wall-time percentiles. That lets you see whether tokenizer differences are causing per-request skew.

3) batch_position: batch_position is the ordinal position in the batch at dequeue time; it is deterministic for the runtime's scheduling policy during that test run but not guaranteed across restarts. For interleaving harnesses capture both batch_id and batch_position; join on request_id to attribute accurately.

Recommended pasteable RFP language (short):

-- Instrumentation fields we expect the vendor to provide (one row per request):
- request_id (string UUID)
- t_client_send (ISO8601)
- t_ingress_recv (ISO8601)
- t_runtime_start (ISO8601)
- t_model_start (ISO8601)
- t_model_end (ISO8601)
- t_runtime_send (ISO8601)
- t_client_recv (ISO8601)
- token_count (int)
- synthetic_probe (boolean)
- synthetic_profile (string)
- cache_hit_count (int)
- batch_id (string)
- batch_position (int)
- model_variant (string)

-- Sampling & aggregation guidance:
- Export per-request traces to a streaming store; compute compute-only and network-only latencies separately.
- Use reservoir sampling for p99.9 when QPS > 20k to bound storage.
- Provide a rolling 1h windowed quantile for operational tail analysis and a full-test-run quantile for gating acceptance criteria.

If helpful we can run a 30-minute cross-harness run with your team and annotate the CSV together. Monica / Ben — can you own scheduling and share the QuickStart CLI command and minimal config? Kimberly — flag any security/ingress constraints for Stratus so we send the proper allowlist.

Thanks — happy to jump on a short 30m call if the SREs want to walk the CSV.

Best,
Aditya

--
Aditya Rao
Head of Solutions — Redwood Inference
aditya@redwood.com
Phone: +1 (415) 555-0192

title: Next steps — model sourcing, benchmarks, and parity gates
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Sarah Lin <sarah.lin@acuity.ai>, Tom Bowers <tbowers@acuity.ai>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 05 Sep 2027 15:22:00 -0700
Subject: Next steps — model sourcing, benchmarks, and parity gates

Hi Sarah / Tom —

Thanks again for the call earlier. Quick recap and a concrete proposal so we can align on the trial and success criteria. High-level notes:

- Primary use case: customer support triage + intent extraction (multi-label) for in-app routing.
- Current baseline: AcuityPro-v3 (your proprietary model) — good precision on top intents but slower inference and some hallucination on entity extraction.
- Goal: determine whether an open model (quantized 13B / 8B variants) can match or beat the baseline on accuracy while improving cost/latency.

Recommendation (trial plan):
1) Run a 2-week sandbox trial on Redwood Hosted with two model families:
   - Open candidate A: Redwood-Open-13B-q4f32 (best accuracy candidate)
   - Open candidate B: Redwood-Open-8B-ptq (lower cost / faster)
   - Fallback: AcuityPro-v3 (baseline) for direct parity comparison
2) We'll run your prompt set (attached) plus our standard NLU evaluation harness.
3) Acceptance gates (parity criteria):
   - Intent accuracy (human-judged macro F1) >= 0.90 relative to current baseline
   - Hallucination/regression: no more than 5% absolute increase in hallucination incidents on our shared prompt set
   - Latency: p95 < 250ms for the 8B candidate, p95 < 400ms for the 13B candidate (on your expected traffic profile)
   - Secondary: token cost delta < +$0.40 per 1k tokens vs baseline to be considered viable for production

Deliverables we will provide:
- Per-prompt evaluation CSV, human judgement rubrics, and side-by-side examples (see attached redwood_benchmark_summary_sep2027.csv).
- Reproducible prompt set (attached: acquity_prompt_set_v1.jsonl).
- Parity acceptance checklist (attached).
- Shared Drive with experiment dashboard: https://drive.google.com/open?id=1aBcdEfBenchmarkSep27

Next steps I propose:
- You confirm the trial window (earliest start Tue 09/07) and any sandbox IP/whitelisting we should configure.
- Sarah: please confirm whether we should treat any subset of intents as high-priority for stricter gating (e.g., payment-related intents).
- Tom: share any constraints on on-prem/VPC testing if you'd prefer we do trial on Dedicated vs Hosted.

If we hit parity on the primary gates, we can scope a 90-day pilot (Dedicated or Private) for live traffic A/B to validate at scale.

Thanks —
Ben
Ben Carter
Senior Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference
[attachment: acquity_prompt_set_v1.jsonl] [attachment: redwood_benchmark_summary_sep2027.csv] [attachment: parity_acceptance_checklist.md]

> Quick tech notes from our side to make the trial less noisy:
>
> - We'll run the same prompt bundle across all candidates with controlled temperature and system messages to avoid prompt-engineering variance.
> - For hallucination detection we will use a combination of heuristic filters + 3 annotator agreement (binary label). I'll attach the rubric to the Drive folder.
> - If an open candidate exceeds hallucination thresholds on >2% of high-priority intents, we will automatically enable per-example fallback to AcuityPro-v3 for those intent buckets during the trial. This avoids user-facing regressions while we iterate.
>
> Action items:
> - Sarah / Tom: confirm the sandbox start (Ben said 09/07) and whether a per-minute rate limit or IP allowlist is required for your side.
> - Ben: share the trial credentials and the initial per-prompt CSV once the first run completes.
> - Karthik: will prepare a short script to compute p95 latency and per-intent F1 from the trial outputs.
>
> Looking forward —
>Marissa
>Marissa Cole
>Staff Applied ML | Redwood Inference
>
>--
>Attachments referenced: acquity_prompt_set_v1.jsonl, redwood_benchmark_summary_sep2027.csv, parity_acceptance_checklist.md
text:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Sarah Lin <sarah.lin@acuity.ai>, Tom Bowers <tbowers@acuity.ai>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 05 Sep 2027 15:22:00 -0700
Subject: Next steps — model sourcing, benchmarks, and parity gates

Hi Sarah / Tom —

Thanks again for the call earlier. Quick recap and a concrete proposal so we can align on the trial and success criteria. High-level notes:

- Primary use case: customer support triage + intent extraction (multi-label) for in-app routing.
- Current baseline: AcuityPro-v3 (your proprietary model) — good precision on top intents but slower inference and some hallucination on entity extraction.
- Goal: determine whether an open model (quantized 13B / 8B variants) can match or beat the baseline on accuracy while improving cost/latency.

Recommendation (trial plan):
1) Run a 2-week sandbox trial on Redwood Hosted with two model families:
   - Open candidate A: Redwood-Open-13B-q4f32 (best accuracy candidate)
   - Open candidate B: Redwood-Open-8B-ptq (lower cost / faster)
   - Fallback: AcuityPro-v3 (baseline) for direct parity comparison
2) We'll run your prompt set (attached) plus our standard NLU evaluation harness.
3) Acceptance gates (parity criteria):
   - Intent accuracy (human-judged macro F1) >= 0.90 relative to current baseline
   - Hallucination/regression: no more than 5% absolute increase in hallucination incidents on our shared prompt set
   - Latency: p95 < 250ms for the 8B candidate, p95 < 400ms for the 13B candidate (on your expected traffic profile)
   - Secondary: token cost delta < +$0.40 per 1k tokens vs baseline to be considered viable for production

Deliverables we will provide:
- Per-prompt evaluation CSV, human judgement rubrics, and side-by-side examples (see attached redwood_benchmark_summary_sep2027.csv).
- Reproducible prompt set (attached: acquity_prompt_set_v1.jsonl).
- Parity acceptance checklist (attached).
- Shared Drive with experiment dashboard: https://drive.google.com/open?id=1aBcdEfBenchmarkSep27

Next steps I propose:
- You confirm the trial window (earliest start Tue 09/07) and any sandbox IP/whitelisting we should configure.
- Sarah: please confirm whether we should treat any subset of intents as high-priority for stricter gating (e.g., payment-related intents).
- Tom: share any constraints on on-prem/VPC testing if you'd prefer we do trial on Dedicated vs Hosted.

If we hit parity on the primary gates, we can scope a 90-day pilot (Dedicated or Private) for live traffic A/B to validate at scale.

Thanks —
Ben
Ben Carter
Senior Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference
[attachment: acquity_prompt_set_v1.jsonl] [attachment: redwood_benchmark_summary_sep2027.csv] [attachment: parity_acceptance_checklist.md]

Hi Ben,

As part of GreenBank's SIG assessment intake, our assessor asked for a compact set of SDLC artifacts related to your code review, CI/CD controls, secrets/keystore handling, and documented change approvals. The assessor is focused on the last 12 months but specifically asked for examples that show enforcement and exception handling.

Requested items (can be redacted where necessary):

Thanks — let me know if you need clarification or a short call.

Alice Wu
Senior Security Assessor
GreenBank Security

title: Adaptive POC rendezvous / proposed schedule & owners
preview:
From: Camila Reyes <camila.reyes@redwood.com>
To: Emma Cole <emma@vertexlogistics.com>, Rahul Singh <rahul@vertexlogistics.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 1 Sep 2027 14:12:00 -0700
Subject: Adaptive POC rendezvous / proposed schedule & owners

Hi Emma / Rahul — great chat earlier, thanks for walking through the use case. This thread is a short post-call recap and a proposed POC schedule so we can lock dates and owners. Please review and flag anything you want to change.

Call highlights (30 min):
- Goal: reduce time-to-ship updates to customers by surfacing relevant shipment exceptions and suggested remedial messages (target: 40% reduction in triage time).
- Data: event stream + enriched metadata from Vertex (sample schema discussed).
- Success criteria (proposed):
  * Precision on suggested messages >= 85% on sampled validation set
  * End-to-end latency for prediction <= 250ms p50 for production path
  * Integration: feature toggled into 10% of traffic for a canary run

Proposed POC timeline (4 weeks):
1) Kickoff week (W0) — alignment + infra prep (propose Tue 2027-09-08, 11:00 PT)
   - Deliverables: dataset access, allowed IP ranges, test service account; owners: Rahul (Vertex) + Marissa (Redwood onboarding)
2) Week 1 — baseline model + smoke tests
   - Deliverables: baseline prompts, sample outputs, integration shim; owner: Marissa
3) Week 2 — quality tuning & evaluation
   - Deliverables: evaluation set, rubric, interim quality report; owner: Camila
4) Week 3 — performance tuning & canary config
   - Deliverables: latency profiling, batching configs, canary rollout plan; owner: Ben
5) Week 4 — final readout + next-step recommendations
   - Deliverables: readout deck, go/no-go recommendation, rough cost estimate; owners: Camila + Rahul

Cadence: weekly 30-minute check-ins (Tuesdays 11:00 PT) + async updates in thread.
Open items / asks for Vertex before kickoff:
- Sample dataset (1 week of events) and data schema
- Security contact for SOC2 questionnaire and IP allowlist
- Preferred test environment and service-account details

Attachments: redwood-adaptive-poc-plan.pdf (detailed runbook), vertex-sow-template.docx (for procurement reference).

If the timeline looks good, I’ll send calendar invites and a cleaned-up runbook.

Thanks —
Camila Reyes
