title: Post-call: multi-hyperscaler delta & unit-econ action plan
preview:
From: Sergio Alvarez <sergio.alvarez@redwood.com>
To: Luke Harper <luke.harper@acme.ai>
Cc: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
Date: Sun, 05 Sep 2027 15:12:00 +0000
Subject: Post-call: multi-hyperscaler delta & unit-econ action plan

Luke / Lauren — thanks again for the call today. Quick recap and proposed next steps so we stay aligned: 

Summary of notes from today
- Primary workload: conversational + short embeddings (avg request ~64 tokens, 70/30 split conv/embed).
- SLOs you called out: p95 latency <= 250ms (interactive), sustained 40–60 QPS at peak.
- Security: you need VPC/private deployment option for the pilot; procurement will ask for SOC2 artifacts.

Competitive / unit-econ snapshot (ballpark, for discussion)
- Redwood (quantized + prefix cache): ~$19 per 1M tokens (assumes 40% cache hit, batching size ~8).
- Hyperscaler A: ~$38 per 1M tokens (no prefix cache for your traffic pattern).
- Hyperscaler B: ~$27 per 1M tokens (better batching but higher latency tail in our tests).

Key technical differentiators we think matter for Acme
- Prefix/KV caching reduces token work for repeated prompts (useful for your 70% repeated intents).
- Continuous batching keeps latency low while improving GPU utilization at your concurrency profile.
- Model variant pinning + automatic fallbacks to cheaper quantized variants for cost predictability.

Concrete next tests we propose
1) 1M token run on your sample dataset (mixed conv+embed) to gather real cache-hit and per-token costs.
2) 30-min stress run at 60 QPS to measure p95 latency and tail behavior with client-like concurrency.
3) Compare identical model bits on Redwood vs Hyperscaler B with same batching and quantization settings; capture token counts and latency histograms.

Attachments: redwood-multi-hyperscaler-delta.pdf (delta table + quick notes), redwood-unit-econ-assumptions.xlsx (assumptions used for the $/1M numbers).

Requests / asks from Acme
- Share a small sample (20–50 representative requests) and an S3 link or upload to the Drive link below so we can seed the cache.
- Confirm whether procurement requires a pre-signed NDA before we run anything on a private endpoint.

I can run the 1M-token job on our side once we have the sample and a short test spec from you (tokenization rules, average prompt length, and target concurrency). If you'd rather, we can give you a short-lived test endpoint and you can run the harness — either works.

Next steps:
- Luke: confirm sample share and security notes.
- Acme procurement: confirm NDA / SSO requirements.
- Redwood: Marissa + Ben to size the lab run and produce a short appendix with assumptions.

Thanks — Sergio

--
Sergio Alvarez
Enterprise Sales, Redwood Inference
sergio.alvarez@redwood.com | +1 (650) 555-0159


From: Luke Harper <luke.harper@acme.ai>
To: Sergio Alvarez <sergio.alvarez@redwood.com>
Cc: Lauren Kim <lauren.kim@acme.ai>
Date: Sun, 05 Sep 2027 19:03:00 +0000
Subject: Re: Post-call: multi-hyperscaler delta & unit-econ action plan

Thanks, Sergio — this is very helpful and captures the call.

We can share a 40-request sample set (anonymized) and a larger S3 bucket with 10k requests for the 1M-token run. I'll check with procurement on the NDA; they usually ask for a one-page pilot MSA. Security-wise, VPC access is preferred but for the initial econ run we can use a short-lived test endpoint if you provide a secure token.

A couple clarifying questions before we hand off to procurement: 
- Can you confirm which model variant you used to produce the $19/1M number? (we want to compare apples-to-apples)
- For the stress run: is there any warmup pattern you recommend to get realistic cache behavior?

I'll get the sample uploaded by EOD tomorrow if procurement doesn't block it.

— Luke
Luke Harper | Head of ML Systems, Acme AI
luke.harper@acme.ai | +1 (415) 555-2222

Date: Mon, 06 Sep 2027 13:11:00 +0000
Subject: Re: Post-call: multi-hyperscaler delta & unit-econ action plan

Sergio — noted. Ben, can you own the lab runs and the unit-econ workbook? I sketched an assumptions tab in redwood-unit-econ-assumptions.xlsx (attached) — please update with real GPU pricing and batching profiles.

Assumptions we should model (placeholders in sheet):
- Model: redwood-instruct-7b-int8 (quantized path) — call this "7b-q" in the sheet.
- Avg request tokens: 64 (prompt + response).
- Cache hit scenarios: 20%, 40%, 60%.
- Batch sizes to simulate: 1, 4, 8, 16.
- Concurrency curves: 10, 30, 60 QPS.

Lab schedule suggestion:
- Wed 09/08 09:00–11:00 PT: run 1M token job + 30-min stress at 60 QPS.
- Wed 09/08 14:00–15:00 PT: quick sync to review outputs and decide hyperscaler comparison knobs.

Ben — if you can populate the sheet with cost per 1M tokens under the different cache/batch combos by EOD Tue, we can share the numbers with Luke ahead of procurement.

Thanks — Marissa

> On Sun, Sep 5, 2027 at 15:12, Sergio Alvarez <sergio.alvarez@redwood.com> wrote:
> > Luke / Lauren — thanks again for the call today. Quick recap and proposed next steps so we stay aligned: 
> > ...


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>, Sergio Alvarez <sergio.alvarez@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 07 Sep 2027 09:02:00 +0000
Subject: Re: Post-call: multi-hyperscaler delta & unit-econ action plan

Marissa — taking this on. Quick preliminary numbers based on the sheet and our current internal pricing: 
- Baseline assumptions: 7b-q, avg 64 tokens, 40% cache hit, batch size 8, GPU amortized cost = $1.20 / hour-equivalent per thousand tokens (internal blended).
- Resulting estimated cost: ~$21 per 1M tokens (internal run, includes infra, networking, and basic cache savings).
- Sensitivity: with 60% cache hit -> ~$15 / 1M tokens; with 20% cache hit -> ~$28 / 1M tokens.

Bench plan checklist I'm using:
- Confirm tokenization matches Acme's pipeline (they mentioned custom tokenizer tweaks).
- Seed cache with 10k representative requests before the 1M run (we get better steady-state numbers).
- Capture latency histograms (p50/p95/p99) and per-call token counts.
- Export raw logs (tokenized requests + response token counts) to the Drive link for audit.

I can populate the workbook and upload updated tabbed runs by EOD Tue. Sergio — do you want me to spin the short-lived test endpoint for Acme (they can hit it directly) or run the lab entirely on our side? I recommend we run on our side first to avoid credentials back-and-forth.

Ben Carter
Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com | +1 (650) 555-0175

Subject: DPA redline and SCCs: transfer addendum questions

Hi Alex —

Thanks for sending over the draft MSA and order form. Our legal team reviewed the DPA and attached a marked-up redline (attached). A few high-priority asks from our side: 

- Controller/Processor role: the redline still treats Brightlane as Controller and Redwood as Processor, but our GC flagged one Order Form clause that could make us joint controllers for certain enrichment flows. Can you confirm current data flow ownership for enrichment and model telemetry?
- SCCs annex: we need the EU SCC Module 2 replacement annex; your draft references an internal exhibit. Please confirm you can sign SCCs Section B (processor addendum) and provide the annex version you intend to execute.
- Transfer Impact Addendum (TIA): given the planned cross-border routing, our privacy lead wants a TIA with a subprocessor inventory and hosting map (regions + fallback). If you can provide a TIA draft we can speed review.
- KMS/key management: Brightlane prefers customer-managed keys for egress-sensitive data. Do you support bring-your-own-key (BYOK) for Dedicated/Private? If not, what mitigations (encryption, HSM) can we commit to?
- Evidence & timeline: can you provide SOC2 scope confirmation (is model training part of scope?) and a 2-week turnaround on a consolidated DPA redline response?

Proposed next step: 30-minute sync with Alex + Redwood legal + Brightlane GC tomorrow or Friday to walk the redlines. We can share an internal tracker if that helps.

Attachment: Brightlane_DPA_redline_v1.docx (docx, 92 KB)

Thanks,
Sarah Wells

From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
To: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Kimberly Park <kimberly.park@redwood.ai>
Date: 2027-09-06T11:30:00-07:00
Subject: RE: DPA redline and SCCs: transfer addendum questions

Alex / team —

I reviewed Brightlane's redline and the asks. High-level position and proposed edits below (I'll prepare a tracked redline and a short memo by EOD):

1) Controller / Processor: our standard model is Brightlane = Controller; Redwood = Processor for inference and embeddings. For enrichment/telemetry (model feature engineering, telemetry logs containing customer PII), we need to narrow the Order Form language to specify that any "data enrichment" work will be performed only on data to which the customer has granted explicit processing rights and that Redwood will process that data as a Processor acting on documented instructions. I propose: add clause in Order Form SOW: "Where Customer supplies data for enrichment, Customer represents it has lawfully collected and may transfer such data and instructs Redwood to act as Processor for the enrichment service; Redwood will not determine the purposes and means of processing."

2) SCCs annex: we can sign the Processor Module (Module 2 equivalent). Recommend attaching the European Commission's 2021 SCCs processor addendum as Exhibit A and include a short table mapping our subprocessors and hosting locations. We should avoid bespoke language that reintroduces controller obligations. I'll prepare the Exhibit with mapped fields.

3) TIA (Transfer Impact Assessment): we should produce a short TIA covering: hosting regions, fallback routing, subprocessors list, encryption in transit & at rest, access controls, and documented audit frequency. I'll draft a 1-page TIA template and populate with our current infra map.

4) KMS / BYOK: BYOK for Dedicated is feasible for Private customer deployments but not for hosted shared tier today. For Dedicated pools we support customer-managed keys via an HSM-backed KMS; we'll need an SC/eng estimate for onboarding . For the Hosted plan we can offer encryption-at-rest + HSM-backed keys managed by Redwood. Kimberly, please provide a clear bullet with: (a) BYOK support yes/no for Dedicated/Private, (b) engineering ETA and any extra run-rate OPEX.

5) SOC 2 scope: confirm that our SOC 2 type II coverage includes production inference endpoints and data storage (not model training sandbox). If model training is in scope, we need to clarify which datasets are in scope. Ops, please confirm scope and provide an SOC2 summary we can share.

6) Proposed timeline: draft TIA + tracked DPA redline by Friday EOD; 30-minute call to walk open points — suggested slots: Fri 10:00-10:30 AM BST or Mon 9:00-9:30 AM BST. Ben — propose final slot to Brightlane.

Attachment: KMS-evidence-notes.txt (txt, 6 KB)

Regards,
Karthik Iyer
Senior Counsel, Redwood Inference

To: Sarah Wells <sarah.wells@brightlane.com>, Brightlane Contracts <contracts@brightlane.com>
Cc: Alex Martinez <alex.martinez@redwood.ai>, Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>

- We're aligned to produce a consolidated redline and a short TIA by Friday EOD. Karthik will lead legal language, Kimberly will confirm BYOK viability and engineering ETA, and Alex will own the infra mapping.
- If Brightlane requires customer-managed keys for production inference, that will likely be scoped as a Dedicated/Private deployment add-on — happy to run numbers and produce a one-pager.
- Proposed call: Friday 10:00-10:30 AM BST — does that work? If so we'll send a calendar invite with dial-in and the draft TIA attached once it's ready.

title: Institutional bulk pricing — research consortium multi-year proposal
preview:
From: Elena Santos <elena.santos@borealinstitute.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2027-09-01T08:12:00Z
Subject: Institutional bulk pricing for consortium

Hi Aisha,

Thanks again for the demo last week. The Boreal Research Consortium (group of 5 universities) is evaluating vendor options for large-scale model inference for cross-site genomics research. We are interested in a multi-year committed program (3 years) with predictable unit pricing and a substantial committed spend in exchange for a deeper discount and a prioritized Dedicated pool.

High level ask: can Redwood provide an "institutional" bulk pricing proposal — target effective discount in the range of 30–40% off list on a 36-month committed spend of approximately $3.2M (annualized ~$1.07M)? Key requirements:
- Dedicated capacity pool with isolated data controls (VPC)
- Quarterly usage reporting and invoice breakdown by research group
- SLA with uptime and latency targets for prioritized jobs
- Standard SOC2/ISO artefacts and an audit clause

I've attached a brief LOI with the expected commitment band and the internal procurement template. Please let me know if you need additional information. We are hoping to get commercial terms and a signable SOW by mid-September so the university boards can review.

Best,
Elena
Director of Procurement
Boreal Research Consortium
Attachments: BRC_LOI_20270901.pdf

Elena —

Appreciate the LOI and the clear requirements. This looks like a strategic fit for our Dedicated offering and for Redwood Optimize recommendations. Quick clarifying questions so I can escalate internally:

I'll draft an internal memo with the recommended commercial structure and send to Finance + RevOps for VP/CFO signoff once I have your confirmation. If you can share a target SLA and any procurement redlines, that will speed things up.

To: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwoodinference.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwoodinference.com>, Grace Oconnor <grace_oconnor@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: 
Date: 2027-09-03T14:02:00Z
Subject: Internal: Boreal Research Consortium — commercial escalation / VP/CFO signoff request

Team —

Summary: Boreal Research Consortium (external contact Elena Santos) requests a 36-month committed Dedicated program (Nov 1 start preferred) with total committed spend ~$3.2M. They are asking for an "institutional" bulk discount target of ~30–40% off list. They require US-only data residency for two partners, SOC2 artefacts, quarterly reporting, and a 99.5% uptime SLA.

Recommended commercial structure (my proposal):
- Term: 36 months, effective Nov 1, 2027
- Commitment: $3.2M total, invoiced quarterly in arrears (or prepaid at customer preference)
- Pricing: 35% effective discount off hosted list for inference token units and embeddings; separate line-item pricing for Dedicated pool reservation fee (to cover reserved GPU baseline)
- Minimum monthly consumption floor: $60k (to prevent churn and ensure reserved capacity economics)
- SLA: 99.5% uptime SLA with standard credit cap (3% of monthly invoice for repeated breach)
- Compliance: provide SOC2 report + lead time for ISO if required; incorporate standard audit clause

Rationale: 35% balances the materiality of a multi-year institutional commitment with our Dedicated capacity economics. The monthly floor preserves utilization on the reserved pool. We can layer in automatic fallback to a slightly lower-priority cluster if they spike beyond committed throughput.

Attachments: attached are my pricing model (Pricing_Playbook_v2.xlsx) and a draft SOW (Draft_SOW_BRC.pdf).

Request: Need VP/CFO signoff to proceed with a formal commercial memo to Procurement and to generate a DocuSign SOW. Please respond with signoff or comments by EOD 9/4 to keep to their mid-September board window. If you want to reduce the discount we should discuss tightening the monthly floor or switching to a 24-month term.

Thanks,
Aisha
Account Executive, Strategic Accounts
Redwood Inference


From: Marissa Cole <marissa_cole@redwoodinference.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwoodinference.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwoodinference.com>, Grace Oconnor <grace_oconnor@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2027-09-04T10:27:00Z
Subject: Re: Internal: Boreal Research Consortium — commercial escalation / VP/CFO signoff request

Aisha —

Thanks for the clear memo. I approve proceeding at the recommended 35% effective discount with the following conditions:
1) Monthly minimum floor of $75k (slightly higher than proposed) to better align with Dedicated baseline costs.
2) Revenue recognition: if they choose prepaid quarterly, we amortize over the quarter; if they default on usage floor twice in a rolling 12 months, we reserve the right to reprice future quarters.
3) SLA credit cap: limit to 3% of monthly invoice and require a maximum of two SLA credits per 12-month period before escalation to operations.
4) Legal: have Legal redline the procurement redlines and ensure our standard indemnity/limitation language remains.
5) Termination: allow a 90-day remediation cure period for repeated material breaches.

Please incorporate these guardrails into the SOW and commercial memo. Marcus, please confirm that the Dedicated capacity plan can be reserved for a Nov 1 start under these economics; Grace, please queue SOC2 artefacts and the ISO pathway with security.

Once Legal provides redlines, Aisha, you can share the draft DocuSign link with Procurement. Approving on the condition that Legal + RevOps accept the protective language above.

—Marissa
Marissa Cole
VP, Commercial Finance
Redwood Inference

Elena —

Good news: Redwood has provisionally approved a 36‑month institutional program for Boreal with the following commercial summary (VP/CFO signoff provided subject to Legal redlines):
- Total commitment: $3.2M over 36 months (Nov 1 start preferred)
- Effective discount: 35% off list (as discussed)
- Monthly minimum floor: $75k
- Dedicated pool reservation: separate charge (included in SOW)
- SLA: 99.5% uptime; credit cap 3% of monthly invoice (see full terms)
- Compliance: SOC2 report will be shared; ISO pathway scoped by Security

Next steps:
1) I'll share a draft SOW and commercial memo with Legal for redlines today (attached).
2) Once Legal signs off, we'll send a DocuSign SOW (HubSpot deal RDW-2027-BRC-0034).
3) Marcus will confirm capacity for a Nov 1 deployment and share a basic runbook for the Dedicated pool.

Please confirm if procurement prefers quarterly prepaid invoices or quarterly invoiced-in-arrears so we can reflect revenue recognition accurately.

From: Elena Santos <elena.santos@borealinstitute.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwoodinference.com>
Date: 2027-09-01T08:12:00Z
Subject: Institutional bulk pricing for consortium

Hi Aisha,

Thanks again for the demo last week. The Boreal Research Consortium (group of 5 universities) is evaluating vendor options for large-scale model inference for cross-site genomics research. We are interested in a multi-year committed program (3 years) with predictable unit pricing and a substantial committed spend in exchange for a deeper discount and a prioritized Dedicated pool.

High level ask: can Redwood provide an "institutional" bulk pricing proposal — target effective discount in the range of 30–40% off list on a 36-month committed spend of approximately $3.2M (annualized ~$1.07M)? Key requirements:
- Dedicated capacity pool with isolated data controls (VPC)
- Quarterly usage reporting and invoice breakdown by research group
- SLA with uptime and latency targets for prioritized jobs
- Standard SOC2/ISO artefacts and an audit clause

I've attached a brief LOI with the expected commitment band and the internal procurement template. Please let me know if you need additional information. We are hoping to get commercial terms and a signable SOW by mid-September so the university boards can review.

Best,
Elena
Director of Procurement
Boreal Research Consortium

Date: 2027-09-03T14:02:00Z
Subject: Internal: Boreal Research Consortium — commercial escalation / VP/CFO signoff request

Team —

Summary: Boreal Research Consortium (external contact Elena Santos) requests a 36-month committed Dedicated program (Nov 1 start preferred) with total committed spend ~$3.2M. They are asking for an "institutional" bulk discount target of ~30–40% off list. They require US-only data residency for two partners, SOC2 artefacts, quarterly reporting, and a 99.5% uptime SLA.

Recommended commercial structure (my proposal):
- Term: 36 months, effective Nov 1, 2027
- Commitment: $3.2M total, invoiced quarterly in arrears (or prepaid at customer preference)
- Pricing: 35% effective discount off hosted list for inference token units and embeddings; separate line-item pricing for Dedicated pool reservation fee (to cover reserved GPU baseline)
- Minimum monthly consumption floor: $60k (to prevent churn and ensure reserved capacity economics)
- SLA: 99.5% uptime SLA with standard credit cap (3% of monthly invoice for repeated breach)
- Compliance: provide SOC2 report + lead time for ISO if required; incorporate standard audit clause

Rationale: 35% balances the materiality of a multi-year institutional commitment with our Dedicated capacity economics. The monthly floor preserves utilization on the reserved pool. We can layer in automatic fallback to a slightly lower-priority cluster if they spike beyond committed throughput.

Attachments: attached are my pricing model (Pricing_Playbook_v2.xlsx) and a draft SOW (Draft_SOW_BRC.pdf).

Request: Need VP/CFO signoff to proceed with a formal commercial memo to Procurement and to generate a DocuSign SOW. Please respond with signoff or comments by EOD 9/4 to keep to their mid-September board window. If you want to reduce the discount we should discuss tightening the monthly floor or switching to a 24-month term.

Thanks,
Aisha

Redwood Inference

Thanks for the clear memo. I approve proceeding at the recommended 35% effective discount with the following conditions:
1) Monthly minimum floor of $75k (slightly higher than proposed) to better align with Dedicated baseline costs.
2) Revenue recognition: if they choose prepaid quarterly, we amortize over the quarter; if they default on usage floor twice in a rolling 12 months, we reserve the right to reprice future quarters.
3) SLA credit cap: limit to 3% of monthly invoice and require a maximum of two SLA credits per 12-month period before escalation to operations.
4) Legal: have Legal redline the procurement redlines and ensure our standard indemnity/limitation language remains.
5) Termination: allow a 90-day remediation cure period for repeated material breaches.

Please incorporate these guardrails into the SOW and commercial memo. Marcus, please confirm that the Dedicated capacity plan can be reserved for a Nov 1 start under these economics; Grace, please queue SOC2 artefacts and the ISO pathway with security.

Once Legal provides redlines, Aisha, you can share the draft DocuSign link with Procurement. Approving on the condition that Legal + RevOps accept the protective language above.

title: Post-call: Brightwell eval scope, model recommendations, and ops next steps
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
To: Theresa Lowe <theresa.lowe@brightwellhealth.com>
Cc: Ryan Kim <ryan.kim@brightwellhealth.com>
Date: 2027-09-01T15:02:00-07:00
Subject: Post-call: Brightwell eval scope, model recommendations, and ops next steps

Hi Theresa — great to speak earlier. Quick recap and proposed next steps so we have a shared plan: 

What we heard
- Goal: automate triage for inbound patient messages and route to correct care team, with extraction of urgent flags.
- Target latency: <300ms p95 for 1–2k QPS; quality threshold: 92% extraction F1 on clinical entities for MVP.

Initial model recommendations (based on today's data profile)
- Candidate A (open-llm-13b-quant): good baseline for intent + extraction; low cost when quantized.
- Candidate B (redwood-tuned-3B-sparse): better for hallucination control on rare clinical terms; recommended for reranking/consistency checks.
- Hybrid approach: primary smaller model + secondary reranker for high-confidence outputs.

Proposed eval plan (short version)
1) We run a 3k sample evaluation on Redwood using your de-identified messages (CSV format, see sample attached).
2) Scoring: extraction F1 + intent accuracy + a simple hallucination rate (manual label on 300 items).
3) Deliverables: ranked model shortlist, per-route latency/cost estimates, and a prompt bank for the top 2 models.

Action items / timeline
- Theresa: share de-identified sample CSV and any existing label schema by EOD Fri (attached example: brightwell-sample-benchmark.csv).
- Ben/Redwood: prepare eval runbook + prompt-set examples and confirm infra access for S3 transfer by Mon 9/6.
- Karthik/SE: propose scoring rubric and dataset format; will loop you in to confirm.

Attachments: brightwell-sample-benchmark.csv (sample schema), prompt-set-examples.md

If this looks right I’ll kick off an internal eval spec and tag Karthik & Marissa for the scoring/runbook work.

Thanks — Ben

-- 
Ben Carter
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.ai | +1 (415) 555-0123

- Scoring: can you include a per-class recall target for our priority entities (medication, symptom, urgency_flag)? We’d like recall > 90 for urgency_flag.
- Timeline: we have a governance window — data needs to hit vendor bucket by 9/8 at latest for legal review. 

Ryan and I will prep a redaction script and a column mapping doc. I’m looping Ryan on this email.

Theresa

Marissa / Karthik — forwarding Brightwell thread. Can you do the following: 

Karthik
- Draft a concise eval spec (dataset format, scoring rubric, sampling guidance). Main asks from customer: per-class recall targets (urgency_flag >90%), extraction F1 target ~92%.
- Include comfort notes for the hybrid model approach and fallback behavior for low-confidence cases.
- Attach any example scoring spreadsheet (CSV) you want them to review.

Marissa
- I’ll need a short runbook for S3 transfer & access controls (customer wants dedicated ACL + separate bucket), plus the internal ops checklist to guarantee deid handling. Also estimate how long the eval run will take and expected cost band.

Theresa / Ryan — Karthik and Marissa are copied; they’ll share the spec and runbook by EOD 9/3. If you have a preferred place to upload (secure share / vendor bucket) please flag.

Thanks — Ben

> On Sep 1, 2027, at 3:02 PM, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai> wrote:
> > (original message omitted)


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
To: Theresa Lowe <theresa.lowe@brightwellhealth.com>, Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>
Date: 2027-09-03T11:22:00-07:00
Subject: RE: Post-call: Brightwell eval spec + scoring rubric (draft)

Theresa — attached is a first-pass eval spec and a scoring CSV template (see attachments in my reply stub). Key points: 

Dataset format (required)
- CSV UTF-8, newline-delimited; columns: message_id, created_at, channel, text, deid_map_id (for our internal audit), label_intent, label_entities(json-string)
- Sample size: 3k primary sample (stratified by channel and urgency), plus 300 targeted hallucination-check items for manual review.

Scoring rubric
- Intent accuracy = correct intent / total (macro accuracy)
- Entity extraction F1 = micro F1 across medication/symptom/urgency_flag (report per-class recall & precision)
- Urgency_flag recall target = 0.90 (90%) — fail if below.
- Hallucination rate = (# responses with fabricated facts) / sampled_manual_checks; target < 3% for top model.

Run/config suggestions on Redwood
- Run 3 candidate configs: open-llm-13b-quant, redwood-tuned-3B-sparse, and a baseline open-7b. Use identical prompt templates; record per-token cost & p95 latency.
- Prefix caching: enable for routing prompts. Batch sizes: conservative (8) for latency SLO, then test up to 32 for throughput tradeoffs.

Deliverables
- Scoring CSV, per-model report (accuracy/F1/hallucination), prompt bank for top two models, and a short playbook for rollout gating (if model regresses).

Notes on privacy & transfer
- We support a vendor-dedicated S3 bucket with restricted ACLs + KMS key. We’ll provide a temporary transfer role and a presigned upload link; we cannot receive raw PHI — tokens must be de-identified before upload.

Attachments referenced: brightwell-eval-spec-v1.csv, scoring-template.csv (attached)

Happy to set a quick call if you want to walk through the rubric.

— Karthik
Principal Solutions Engineer, Redwood Inference
karthik.iyer@redwood.ai


From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.ai>

Date: 2027-09-04T10:15:00-07:00
Subject: RE: Post-call: Brightwell eval spec + scoring rubric (ops & next steps)

1) Data ingest & security- We will provision an S3 prefix in a dedicated bucket (redwood-brightwell-evals) with customer-specific KMS. You will get a short-lived IAM role and a presigned PUT link for uploads. Legal needs: we require the deid confirmation (checkbox) and the mapping document for audit (redact_map.tsv).

2) Timeline & handoffs- Customer upload deadline: 2027-09-08 23:59 PT (per Theresa request).- Internal review & sample validation: 9/9–9/10.- First eval run window (3k sample): 9/11–9/12, deliverable on 9/13 (model report + prompt bank).

3) Cost estimate (ballpark)
- Expected compute band for 3-model run: $2.2k–$3.5k depending on exact batch/quant settings. We’ll provide a final estimate with the eval spec.

4) Attachments for your review- redwood-eval-runbook-v1.pdf (ops checklist), prompt-set-examples.md (prompt templates), brightwell-sample-benchmark.csv (schema sample).

Next steps: Theresa/Ryan upload de-identified CSV and mapping doc to the provided presigned link by 9/8. Karthik will finalize the scoring CSV and we’ll schedule a 30-min sync if any mapping items are unclear.

If anything here looks off, flag it and we’ll adjust. Otherwise we’ll assume the 9/8 upload cutoff and proceed.

Thanks — Marissa
Senior Customer Success Manager, Redwood Inference

title: Prospect Q&A: data handling locations, subprocessors & deletion guarantees
preview:
From: Priya Suresh <priya.s@acmehealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 01 Sep 2027 09:10:00 -0700
Subject: Quick Q on where ACME data would be processed

Hi Ben,

Thanks for the demo yesterday — ACME Health is excited about Redwood's latency and cost story. Before we take this further through procurement, our security team needs a concise answer to two items so we can scope a legal review:

1) Which geographic regions (cloud regions and Redwood-owned locations) will customer request payloads and model outputs traverse or be processed in for the Hosted API? We care about any temporary sojourns or cross-border egress.

2) What's your standard deletion guarantee for request/response logs, embeddings and KV caches? We need confirmation of timelines and an option for a stricter retention window.

Can you provide a short Q&A and any evidence (diagram, SOC2 page) we can include in our security packet?

Best,
Priya
Priya Suresh
Security Engineer, ACME Health
security@acmehealth.com



From: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
To: Priya Suresh <priya.s@acmehealth.com>
Cc: Monica Patel <monica@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 01 Sep 2027 11:00:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Quick Q on where ACME data would be processed

Hi Priya — great to hear and thanks for following up. Looping in Monica from our security team to provide the technical detail and the evidence pack.

A couple quick clarifying points for ACME so Monica can tailor the response:
- Will you be using the Hosted API (redwood-managed) or planning a Dedicated/Private deployment? Hosted answers below assume redwood-managed endpoints.
- Do you anticipate PHI in requests, or is the traffic limited to identifiers and non-sensitive metadata?

If you'd prefer, we can do a 30-min call to walk through the map and the deletion options — Monica is available tomorrow afternoon.

Thanks,
Ben Carter
Account Executive, Redwood Inference
---
On Wed, Sep 1, 2027 at 09:10 AM Priya Suresh <priya.s@acmehealth.com> wrote:
> (original message)


From: Monica Patel <monica@redwood.com>
To: Priya Suresh <priya.s@acmehealth.com>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben@redwood.com>
Date: Thu, 02 Sep 2027 09:02:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Quick Q on where ACME data would be processed

Hi Priya — Monica from Security here. Thanks for the clear questions; answers below assume ACME uses the Hosted API in US regions. If you plan Dedicated or Private, we will provide a deployment-specific map.

1) Regions and processing path (short answer):
- In the Hosted API default (US), requests enter our US ingress (primary regions: us-west-2 or us-east-1 depending on routing policy).
- TTL/ephemeral processing may route requests to worker pools in either region based on capacity and latency SLOs. We do not route requests to third countries by default.
- Cross-border transfer: if a customer explicitly configures routing to EU/APAC regions, or pins a model in those regions, request processing will occur there. For Healthcare customers requiring no cross-border processing, we offer Dedicated and Private deployment options (VPC or on-prem).
- Subprocessors: primary cloud providers (AWS in the US) and our CDN provider for TLS termination. We maintain a subprocessors inventory; see the attached processing map for current locations and provider names.

2) Retention and deletion (short answer):
- Request/response logs: default retention 30 days; can be reduced to 7 days for enterprise contracts. We offer an option to request immediate purge of logs for specific request IDs with proof of ownership; typical compliance window is 72 hours to complete purge and provide evidence.
- Embeddings and derived artifacts: default retention 90 days; enterprise customers can opt into shorter retention or disable long-term storage entirely.
- KV cache/prefix caches: ephemeral by design for performance; default TTL ~24 hours, but no long-term storage unless explicitly enabled.
- Audit trail: deletion requests generate an audit entry; we can provide a signed deletion certificate on request for Dedicated and Private customers.

Evidence and caveats:
- Attached: redwood-processing-map-Q3-2027.pdf (diagram with region hops and subprocessors) and SOC2-summary-2026.pdf (control summary).
- Export controls / legal holds: deletion may be delayed if required by a valid legal hold or law enforcement request. We will notify the account security contact in such cases.
- Data used for model improvement: by default we do not use customer payloads for model training without explicit opt-in; this can be contractually disallowed.

If ACME needs a written SLA clause or a custom retention matrix, happy to put together contract language for procurement review. Would you like the 30-min call to go over the map? I can bring our Compliance engineer if you want deeper ATO-level detail.

Regards,
Monica Patel
Senior Security Engineer, Redwood Inference
Attachments: redwood-processing-map-Q3-2027.pdf (diagram), SOC2-summary-2026.pdf (control summary)

Ben — short answer: send the reissued package and the bridge. Explanation below so you can paste it into the reply to BluePeak.

What happened: during the 2026 assessment the auditor agreed to an extended observation window for a service migration that started 2026-06-01. The original Type II package (package ID: RW-SOC2-2026-v1) was published in early 2027 with the calendar 2026 window (2026-01-01 to 2026-12-31). After post-audit changes and acceptance from the auditor, we reissued an amended package that adjusts the audited-observation period to 2026-06-01 through 2027-05-31 to better reflect the migration coverage and evidence continuity. The reissued document is the authoritative artifact for control coverage; the original v1 remains archived for traceability.

title: Advanced dashboards carveout & tracing fee — counteroffer and pilot credit
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Priya Singh <priya.singh@nimbushealth.com>, Raj Patel <raj@cloudprocure.io>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Mon, 30 Aug 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Observability add‑ons: carveout approach + initial numbers attached

Hi Priya / Raj —

Thanks for the time yesterday. Attached is a concise packaging proposal that separates our core API commit from the observability add‑ons (retention, tracing ingest, and advanced dashboards) so you can see the unit economics clearly. Key points up front: 

- We can carve out the advanced dashboards from the seat-based entitlements so they won’t count against your primary user seats. That helps when clinical analytics seats spike.
- Tracing ingest will be metered; we can offer a capped tier for the pilot to limit surprise bills.
- We’ve included two commercial levers: a one‑time implementation credit and a volume discount tier on add‑ons that scales by average monthly ingest.

See pricing_grid_nimbus_v4.xlsx for the line‑item view and the attached SOW_Nimbus_Observability_Carveout_v2.pdf for scope.

Ask: can you confirm whether the carveout meets the procurement/risk needs (i.e., dashboards excluded from seat counts), and share a target annual add‑on spend so I can model the discount curve?

If it helps we can propose a 90‑day pilot with a $10k credit against the add‑ons while you exercise queries and trace patterns.

Thanks — I’ll stand by for the budgeting signal and will loop legal once we have alignment on carveout language.

Best,
Ben Carter
Senior Solutions Executive
Redwood Inference
(attachment: SOW_Nimbus_Observability_Carveout_v2.pdf)
(attachment: pricing_grid_nimbus_v4.xlsx)

From: Priya Singh <priya.singh@nimbushealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Raj Patel <raj@cloudprocure.io>
Date: Tue, 31 Aug 2027 11:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Observability add‑ons: carveout approach + initial numbers attached

Ben —

Appreciate the quick turn. A few clarifying items from procurement and engineering: 

1) By “carve out the advanced dashboards”, we mean they should not be tied to our licensed clinician seats or push us into higher seat tiers during usage spikes. If dashboards are standalone SKU that’s ideal.

2) Tracing ingest: we like the idea of a capped pilot tier. Can you confirm what the cap looks like (GB/day) and the overage rate? We had something like 500GB/day in mind for initial POC.

3) Commercial ask: Nimbus has an internal guidance to get to at least a 20% discount on add‑ons for any new vendor at contract signing if we commit 12 months. If Redwood can’t reach 20% across the board, is there room for a split approach (20% on dashboards + 10% on tracing, or similar)?

4) We’ll need carveout language in the SOW that explicitly states dashboards will be invoiced separately and will not affect seat counts.

Happy to jump on a short call if it’s easier.

—Priya
Director, Procurement
Nimbus Health

(attachment: nimbus_commercial_requirements.pdf)

From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
To: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>, Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Tue, 31 Aug 2027 13:20:00 -0700
Subject: Fwd: Observability add‑ons: carveout approach + initial numbers attached

Folks — forwarding Priya's notes. Quick ask: procurement is pushing for 20% discount on add‑ons if Nimbus commits 12 months. They explicitly want dashboards carved out from seat math and a capped tracing pilot (500GB/day).

My initial thinking: 
- Dashboards: we can offer standalone dashboard SKU and give 20% off if they sign 12‑month commitment for dashboards AND the core commit stays at our standard rate.
- Tracing: instead of 20% off, propose a 10% discount + three months of pilot credits equivalent to $12k (to offset expected ingest costs while they tune instrumentation). Overage rate set at $0.08/GB after cap during pilot.

Pricing impact summary (back‑of‑envelope): 20% on dashboard list = ~$48k ARR reduction vs list; tracing concession = ~$9k ARR concession for first year assuming their pilot becomes steady. We can offset with a clause: 6‑month review to adjust discounts if usage materially exceeds projections.

Would love Marissa’s signoff on range and Jordan to confirm whether $0.08/GB is allowable on the tracing side. Also legal needs a clear carveout clause for the SOW — I can draft and send once we align.

Ben

From: Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwoodinference.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwoodinference.com>
Cc: Jordan Blake <jordan.blake@redwoodinference.com>
Date: Wed, 1 Sep 2027 09:08:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Fwd: Observability add‑ons: carveout approach + initial numbers attached

Ben —

Thanks for the context. I think we can meet them in the middle with a commercially clean offer that preserves economics: 

Proposal to present: 
- Advanced dashboards: standalone SKU, 20% discount off list if Nimbus signs a 12‑month commitment for the dashboards SKU. Contractually we’ll add an addendum that dashboards are invoiced separately and excluded from seat counts.

- Tracing ingest: capped pilot (500GB/day) with the following commercials: 10% discount on list price for the first 12 months + a pilot credit of $15,000 applied to the first invoice. Overage after pilot: $0.09/GB (slightly higher than Ben's spreadsheet but gives us margin). We’ll include a 6‑month review clause to revisit ingest economics.

- Implementation/onboarding: waive the first month of dashboard licensing (equivalent credit) if Nimbus executes within 14 days. That reduces friction for procurement.

Rationale: Hitting 20% on dashboards keeps procurement happy; the tracing concession is meaningful without setting a deep, long‑term price precedent. The $15k credit is within our deal desk limits for this ARR.

Jordan — confirm the $0.09/GB overage and that legal can create a succinct carveout addendum. If you approve, Ben, I’ll update the pricing_grid_nimbus_v4.xlsx (attached) and return an updated SOW for signature.

Marissa
VP, Enterprise Sales
Redwood Inference
(attachment: pricing_grid_nimbus_v4_updated.xlsx)

Priya / Raj —

Thanks again for the candid asks. After a quick internal review we’re prepared to proceed with the following commercial package: 

1) Advanced dashboards: standalone SKU, 20% discount off list price with a 12‑month minimum commitment for dashboards. Dashboards are explicitly excluded from seat counts in the attached SOW addendum.

2) Tracing: 500GB/day capped pilot for 90 days. During the pilot we’ll apply a $15,000 credit toward tracing charges and provide 10% off list for months 1–12. Overage after the pilot will be billed at $0.09/GB. We’ll include a 6‑month usage review to confirm steady‑state pricing.

3) Implementation: first month of dashboard licensing waived (applied as credit) if the SOW is countersigned within 14 days.

Attachments: SOW_Nimbus_Observability_Carveout_v2.pdf (updated with carveout addendum), pricing_grid_nimbus_v4_updated.xlsx, implementation_plan.docx

Next steps: 
- Please confirm acceptance by replying to this thread. We will push the SOW to DocuSign (link below) once you confirm commercials.

- DocuSign (preview): https://www.docusign.com/en/sign/abcdef123456

If you’d prefer a quick 20‑minute call to walk through the SOW I can join with Marissa and Jordan.

Looking forward to getting Nimbus live.

Best,

Redwood Inference

title: Re: Latency commitment request for enterprise contract — Neuronix
preview:
From: Ellis Thompson <ellis@neuronix.ai>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 30 Aug 2027 09:12:00 -0700
Subject: Latency commitment request for enterprise contract

Hi Aisha,

Thanks again for walking us through Redwood's Dedicated offering last week. As we finalize procurement docs for the enterprise contract, our execs have asked for a formal latency commitment for the production workload. High level:

- Traffic profile: 1k QPS peak (text generation, average prompt length ~180 tokens)
- Request: p95 generation latency <= 90ms and overall service uptime >= 99.95% for US region
- Timeline: goal to sign and go live by Oct 1; willing to discuss a staged ramp/commitment if needed

We attached an updated proposal with the commercial numbers and tentative traffic ramp. Can Redwood confirm whether you can put a sub-90ms p95 latency commitment into an appendix for Neuronix's SLA? If not, what alternate commitments or constraints (dedicated capacity, traffic shaping, warm pools) would you propose?

Appreciate a call if that's easier — Ellis (cc'ed contracts@neuronix.ai).

Attachment: [neuronix_proposal_v3.pdf]

Best,
Ellis Thompson
Head of Platform Engineering, Neuronix.ai
ellis@neuronix.ai
+1 (415) 555-0191


From: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>
To: Dev Patel <dev_patel@redwood.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace_oconnor@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>
Cc: 
Date: Mon, 30 Aug 2027 10:05:00 -0700
Subject: Fwd: Latency commitment request for enterprise contract

Team — forwarding Ellis's ask from Neuronix (external). They're requesting p95 <= 90ms at ~1k QPS and 99.95% uptime for US. They want an appendix in the contract.

Quick asks:
- Product/engineering: feasibility and minimal technical constraints we would need to guarantee p95<=90ms (dedicated pool, model variant, quantization, traffic shaping).
- SRE/ops: runbook and operational controls, how we'd measure/verify latency for SLA reporting; what monitoring + synthetic tests are required.
- Sales: commercial levers (dedicated premium, capacity reservation, penalties). Marcus, please advise on what we can offer commercially while protecting ops.

Timing: Neuronix is aiming to sign by Oct 1. Can I get an internal summary from each of you by EOD Wednesday (Sept 1)? If we push back, I'll propose an alternative SLA structure (e.g., p95 <= 120ms standard + a paid low-latency add-on).

From: Dev Patel <dev_patel@redwood.com>
To: Aisha Rahman <aisha_rahman@redwood.com>, Grace O'Connor <grace_oconnor@redwood.com>, Marcus Lin <marcus_lin@redwood.com>
Date: Tue, 31 Aug 2027 09:18:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Fwd: Latency commitment request for enterprise contract

Hi all,

Quick technical read: sub-90ms p95 at 1k QPS for generation is extremely aggressive unless we constrain a few dimensions. High level feasibility and trade-offs below.

What makes sub-90ms hard:
- Model choice & context length: larger models and longer prompts add variance.
- Cold vs warm state: LRU cache misses and KV cache population cause spikes.
- Hardware contention: multi-tenant GPU pools introduce jitter.

Feasible approaches (in descending order of operational cost):
1) Dedicated reserved pool (recommended path)
   - Dedicated mulligan: provision N GPUs exclusively for Neuronix traffic + pre-warmed KV cache and pinned model weights (hot cache)
   - Expected p95: <=90ms for typical prompts if we limit sequence length to <=256 tokens and use the quantized low-latency model variant (we have validated traces for similar customers).
   - Trade-offs: ~2.1x incremental hosting cost vs shared hosted; needs a committed monthly baseline (we should quantify in $).

2) Traffic routing + hybrid fallback
   - Route 60-70% of traffic to low-latency variant on dedicated nodes; overflow to standard nodes with p95<=140ms.
   - Pros: lower cost than full dedicated. Cons: complexity + degraded experience on overflow.

3) Contract-level constraints rather than strict number
   - Offer p95<=120ms as a guaranteed SLA and an optional low-latency premium add-on for p95<=90ms tied to a dedicated pool.

Operational requirements (minimum):
- SLO definition doc: exact measurement windows, warmup handling, synthetic traffic spec.
- Canary and staged rollouts for model version changes.
- Capacity reservation + autoscaling guardrails.

Engineering effort estimates: small-medium changes to provisioning + SLA telemetry (~3-4 weeks of infra/product effort to add formal metrics + canary jobs and automate warm pools), plus SRE runbook (~1 week). Cost delta: estimate 1.8x-2.5x depending on GPU class and replication.

Attachment: [latency-analysis-2027-08.xlsx] (summary in body above)

Happy to jump on a technical call with Neuronix to align constraints.

— Dev
Dev Patel
Senior Product Manager, Serving Runtime
dev_patel@redwood.com


From: Grace O'Connor <grace_oconnor@redwood.com>

Date: Wed, 01 Sep 2027 11:31:00 -0700
Subject: Re: Fwd: Latency commitment request for enterprise contract

Adding ops POV.

If we go the dedicated-pool route we will need the following before we can sign any hard SLA:
- Definitive SLO language (measurement method, windowing, exclusion policies for maintenance and force majeure).
- A synthetic workload and monitoring dashboard we control and the customer acknowledges as the source of truth for SLA measurement.
- A runbook covering: capacity pre-warm, cache warming guidance, incident escalation, and RTO/RPO for model reloads.
- On-call staffing commitments for high-touch accounts (this increases our OPEX and needs to be priced).

Operational risks and mitigations:
- Deployment & model-change regressions: require canary for every model update and an automatic fallback to a compatible model variant if latency degrades.
- Region-level failures: consider cross-region failover but exclude it from the latency guarantee (we can offer uptime as cross-region).

I will draft a short SLO + runbook template for Neuronix and share by EOD Thu. Also note we need legal to vet penalty language (Marcus to loop in Legal).

From: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>

Subject: ProspectHealth — DPA/SCCs: transfer‑impact annex + MSA/order form redlines (starter)

Team — starting a dedicated thread for the ProspectHealth legal redlines. They returned the MSA + order form with two sets of asks: (1) updated SCCs language and (2) a transfer‑impact annex for their EU controllers. We need legal/ops alignment before I loop in procurement.

High level: they want (a) explicit subprocessor routing clauses, (b) a narrow export controls carveout, and (c) an annex outlining regional routing and encryption at rest obligations. They flagged a couple of MSA sections for indemnity & liability to be rebalanced.

Attachments: ProspectHealth_MSA_OrderForm_v2_redline.docx (their redline), SCCs_Clauses_Proposal.pdf (our suggested SCCs inserts), transfer-impact-annex-draft.docx (initial draft for discussion).

Requests:
- Karthik: can you review the SCCs inserts and confirm whether we can accept the "controller-to-controller" phrasing they insisted on? That affects our handing of subprocessor flow‑downs.
- Laura: please call out any MSA liability exposures that need escalation to leadership.
- Ben: ops/infra questions — can we meet the regional routing constraints without an addl infra change? If not, estimate scope.

Goal: get a clean internal redline and Qs to send to their GC by EOD tomorrow (Thu 09/02).

Thanks,
Ava

--
Ava Chen
Account Executive, Strategic Sales
Redwood Inference
ava.chen@redwood.ai | +1 (415) 555‑0112


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.ai>
To: Ava Chen <ava.chen@redwood.ai>, Laura Bennett <laura.bennett@redwood.ai>
Cc: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.ai>
Date: Wed, 1 Sep 2027 10:03:00 -0700
Subject: Re: ProspectHealth — DPA/SCCs: transfer‑impact annex + MSA/order form redlines (starter)

Ava — quick notes after scanning the attachments:

- SCCs_Clauses_Proposal.pdf: overall approach OK, but the "controller-to-controller" label is problematic. We should avoid categorizing our service as a controller for customer data unless there's a clear business justification — that opens us to controller obligations we don't want. Recommend keeping processor language and adding a narrow clause allowing customers to require limited controller‑to‑controller treatment for specific data types where they remain the controller (e.g., certain identity attributes).
- transfer-impact-annex-draft.docx: good start. Need to add: (i) explicit fallback to AES‑256 for at‑rest encryption, (ii) an operational runbook entry on cross‑border request handling, and (iii) a subprocessor approval matrix (3 tiers).
- MSA redlines: the indemnity shift they requested (Section 7.4) is too broad. Laura should propose capped liability linked to direct damages and carve out infra failures due to customer misconfiguration.

I can draft suggested language for SCCs and the annex by EOD today.

Karthik

--
Karthik Iyer
Director, Privacy & Legal Ops
karthik.iyer@redwood.ai

Top Qs for you / Jake:
1) On the controller‑to‑controller ask: can you flag which data categories you view as controller‑to‑controller? That scope will determine acceptable wording (full controller status vs. limited treatment).
2) For the transfer‑impact annex: ProspectHealth asks for country‑specific routing commitments (e.g., EU‑only processing for PH patient profiles). Is there any flexibility to accept a contractual commitment to use EU regions by default with the right to use non‑EU regions for defined exceptions (disaster recovery, regional outages)?
3) Subprocessor transparency: we'll provide a tiered approval matrix. Can you confirm whether you need prior approval for Tier 2 subprocessors or if a notice + objection window (10 business days) is sufficient?

We attached our suggested SCCs inserts and a transfer‑impact annex draft. We'll propose narrower indemnity language in the MSA.

Proposed next step: sync call Thu 09/02 at 10am CEST / 1am PDT? (If that doesn't work for you, propose another time.)

Ava

Subject: Re: ProspectHealth — DPA/SCCs: transfer‑impact annex + MSA/order form redlines (starter)

Thanks Ava — appreciate the quick coordination. Answers inline: 

1) Controller‑to‑controller: our privacy team views the following categories as needing controller‑to‑controller treatment: patient identifiers, PHI metadata, and billing identifiers. For other telemetry/usage logs, we accept processor status. We'll provide a short schedule enumerating the categories by close of business today.

2) EU‑only processing: we can accept contractual commitment to EU‑first processing for patient profiles, with exceptions limited to documented failover events (region outage, disaster recovery). We'd like SLA visibility on DR events and an incident report within 48 hours when failover is used.

3) Subprocessor approvals: due to regulatory reasons, we need prior approval for any Tier 2 subprocessors that will process patient identifiers or PHI. For other subprocessors, notice + 10 business days objection window is acceptable.

Jake (cc'd) will circulate a redline of the annex and SCCs inserts with comments. We're open to the indemnity cap discussion — can you propose a numeric cap tied to the order form value?

Available for the proposed call Thu 09/02 at 10:00 CEST.

Regards,
Sarah

ProspectHealth

Date: Thu, 2 Sep 2027 09:22:00 -0700
Subject: Re: ProspectHealth — infra/ops constraints for EU‑first routing & subproc flows

Folks — quick ops check after Sarah's note: 

- EU‑first routing for patient profiles is feasible from a provisioning and tenancy standpoint. We need to catalog the customer dataset mapping so our router can pin data to eu‑west clusters. That's a one‑time mapping effort + small config update per account (2–3 engineer days).
- Disaster recovery failover to non‑EU is supported but requires automations to tag the event in our audit logs and trigger the incident report flow Sarah asks for. We'll need to add a flag in the incident pipeline (1 engineer day).
- Subprocessor approvals: Tier 2 prior‑approval workflow will require a short procurement change (UI + SLA flow) — estimate ~2 weeks to ship for self‑serve approval; we can handle manual approvals in the interim.

If we push the EU‑first requirement live, we should add language in the annex giving us limited operational latitude for DR events and define the incident report SLA. I can draft a short operations paragraph for the annex.

Ben

title: Asteria Health — dedicated capacity hardware tradeoffs & perf validation
preview:
From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Sophie Turner <sophie@asteriahealth.com>, Mike Daniels <mike.daniels@asteriahealth.com>
Cc: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Mon, 30 Aug 2027 15:14:00 -0700
Subject: Recap + proposed perf validation plan (dedicated capacity)

Hi Sophie / Mike —

Thanks again for the call today. Quick recap and propose next steps so we keep momentum:

Key SLOs you gave us on the call:
- p95 generation latency: <= 150 ms for streaming responses (token-by-token)
- sustained concurrency target: 1,200 simultaneous active sessions
- peak sustained throughput target: ~60k tokens/sec (aggregate across pool)
- typical response size: median 60 tokens, 95th percentile up to 800 tokens

Constraints / notes you mentioned:
- majority of requests are streaming-rich (progressive partial tokens) and low CPU in pre/post processing
- strict data retention rules in Procure (30 days ephemeral logs) — we will honor in Private/Dedicated setup

Proposed validation plan (brief):
1) Baseline test: run your prompt set (attached: asteria_prompt_set_20270830.txt) against a synthetic traffic generator that simulates your concurrency and token distribution.
2) HW comparison: benchmark two reserved-capacity configs: (A) A100 80GB pool, (B) H100 L40 / H100 80GB pool (depending on availability). We'll report p50/p95/p99 streaming latencies, throughput, and GPU utilization.
3) Profiles: test with/without int8 quantization and two batching policies (low-latency small batches vs throughput-optimized).
4) Mutual action plan: schedule a 2-day validation window; we do the bench, you review logs and confirm acceptance criteria.

Immediate asks from your side:
- Confirm the SLO numbers above are correct or flag changes.
- Share any internal test prompts we should prioritize (if different from attached set).
- Any network egress restrictions or IP allowlist we should prepare for (we can use our Private/Dedicated onboarding checklist).

If that looks good I'll loop Karthik to schedule the bench and Marissa to draft the MAP / procurement timeline. Rough ETA for bench results: 3 business days after we get your final prompt set and allowlist.

Thanks —
Ben
Ben Carter
Enterprise Solutions Engineer | Redwood Inference
ben.carter@redwood.com
attachments: asteria_prompt_set_20270830.txt


From: Sophie Turner <sophie@asteriahealth.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Cc: Mike Daniels <mike.daniels@asteriahealth.com>
Date: Mon, 30 Aug 2027 18:05:00 -04:00
Subject: Re: Recap + proposed perf validation plan (dedicated capacity)

Thanks Ben — that summary looks right and aligned with what we discussed. A few clarifications: 

- The 150 ms p95 is for token-streaming latency measured from the first response byte to each subsequent token emission (we understand p50 will be much lower). If you measure end-to-end generation per message (last token), please also report that p95 for completeness.
- Concurrency: 1,200 active sessions is a steady-state target. We expect short spikes up to 1,800 for ~5–10 minutes during business hours.
- Token mix: ~65% of requests are short (<100 tokens), 25% are mid (100–400), and 10% are long (400–800).

Also attaching a short doc with our network constraints (network_constraints.pdf) — we prefer a private backbone peering option if available for the dedicated environment.

Two asks for you:
1) Please include an estimated unit cost delta (per-1M tokens or per-hour reserved) for each HW option in the bench results.
2) Confirm timing: can you start the bench on Wednesday (Sep 1)? We can provide an allowlist and final prompt tweaks by EOD Tuesday.

Appreciate it — Sophie
Sophie Turner
Head of AI Platform | Asteria Health
sophie@asteriahealth.com


From: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>
To: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Cc: Sophie Turner <sophie@asteriahealth.com>, Mike Daniels <mike.daniels@asteriahealth.com>
Date: Mon, 30 Aug 2027 19:02:00 -07:00
Subject: Internal: Asteria bench plan + asks (please action)

Karthik / Marissa — moving this to internal channel so we can align execution. Sophie confirmed numbers and wants a cost delta + ability to start bench on Wed Sep 1 (client will provide allowlist by EOD Tue).

Planned validation matrix (high level):
- Hardware variants: A100 80GB (perf mode) vs H100 80GB (mixed-precision + triton kernels). If H100 L40 is the cloud SKU offered by partner, include that as a third column.
- Model config: their model archetype = 13B open-weight variant (we're validating for a 13B-ish family); test with fp16 and int8 q4_0 where supported.
- Batch / scheduling: two batch policies: low-latency (target token latency < 150ms p95) with dynamic batching disabled vs throughput (aggressive batching, continuous batching windows of 50–100ms).
- Targets to capture: p50/p95/p99 streaming latency (token emission), end-to-end completion p95, GPU util, tokens/sec, mem pressure, swap events, KV cache miss ratio.

Deliverables: perf_matrix_template.xlsx (attached) + short writeup with a recommended reserved throughput (tokens/sec), recommended pool size (number of GPUs), and estimated monthly cost delta for each HW option.

Karthik — can you run the bench per matrix and prioritize streaming token emit p95 results? Focus on:
- a) 1,200 steady concurrency profile; b) 1,800 spike for 10 minutes; c) long-tail requests up to 800 tokens to exercise KV cache.
- Please capture per-GPU latency distribution and suggestions on batching window size to hit 150ms p95.

Marissa — can you start a mutual action plan draft and procurement timeline (include DocuSign path & expected SOC2 artifact handoff) so Sophie has procurement visibility?

I'll own coordinating the client allowlist and running the synthetic traffic harness.

Ben


From: Karthik Iyer <karthik.iyer@redwood.com>
To: Ben Carter <ben.carter@redwood.com>, Marissa Cole <marissa.cole@redwood.com>
Date: Wed, 1 Sep 2027 11:02:00 -07:00
Subject: Re: Internal: Asteria bench plan + asks (please action)

Thanks Ben — acknowledged. Quick timeline and notes: 

Timeline:
- Wed Sep 1 (today): ingest prompt set + allowlist; spin up test pools (A100 / H100).
- Thu Sep 2: run steady-state 1,200 concurrency bench + 1,800 spike window; collect streaming token emit traces.
- Fri Sep 3: run long-tail stress (95th/99th token sizes), finalize perf matrix and cost deltas; hand to Ben/Marissa for client writeup.

Initial expectations (subject to bench data):
- A100 80GB: expected p95 token-emit latency ~130–170 ms under low-latency batching at 1,200 conc. Throughput per GPU ~6–8k tokens/sec depending on model and batch policy.
- H100 80GB: expected p95 token-emit latency ~110–150 ms under same policy; throughput per GPU ~8–10k tokens/sec.
- Quantized int8: reduces GPU memory pressure and can increase per-GPU tokens/sec by ~15–30% but may add 5–20ms tail latency on long contexts — we'll quantify.

Bench notes / technical asks:
- We'll instrument token emit timing at the runtime layer and at the network egress to separate runtime from networking.
- Request that Sophie include a short sample of the longest completions (400–800 tokens) to ensure offload behavior is covered.

I'll attach the raw perf CSVs and the filled perf_matrix_template.xlsx to the hubspot link when done.

Karthik
Principal Performance Engineer | Redwood Inference
